title: Chapter 1: Death by Delivery
content:
# The Abyss Rancher: How I Saved the World by Farming
## Chapter 1: Death by Delivery

---

Mu Chen died the way most delivery drivers in Chengdu die — by saving three minutes on a red light.

The truck came from the left, ran the yellow, didn't even honk. His electric scooter became a compressed accordion of aluminum and cheap plastic. Mu Chen flew three meters through air that tasted like exhaust and rain, and his skull kissed the curb with a sound like a watermelon being dropped from a third-floor balcony.

He lay on the asphalt for what felt like forever, watching the sky turn a beautiful, expensive shade of crimson. The last thing he saw was his cracked phone, still glowing, displaying the food delivery app.

"Order #4782 — Distance to delivery: 800 meters. Estimated tip: ¥3."

Three yuan. Forty cents.

He thought, absurdly, that the customer would mark him late. That his rating would drop below 4.8. That tomorrow morning, the algorithm would send him fewer orders. That by next month, he'd be kicked off the platform. That he'd have to go back to his parents' apartment in Meishan and listen to his mother cry about how he, the only one of her three sons to actually finish college, was now delivering food at thirty.

He thought about all of that.

Then he stopped thinking.

---

When Mu Chen opened his eyes, the first thing he noticed was the cold.

Not Chengdu-winter cold. Not air-conditioning-on-too-high cold. This was the kind of cold that lived in the bones, the kind that said *you are somewhere you should not be.*

The second thing he noticed was the smell. Rot. Decay. And something else, something sweet, like overripe fruit mixed with ozone.

The third thing he noticed was something wet and rough dragging across his face.

He sat up so fast he almost threw up. His hands — *his hands, still in his hands, ok* — slapped at his cheek, smearing away whatever had been licking him.

He looked at his palm.

It was green.

A black-green ichor, thick as honey, with strands of something that looked suspiciously like *moss* woven through it.

Mu Chen scrambled backward on his ass, feet kicking, and only then did he take in the world around him.

Gray sky. Gray earth. A horizon that was just... *gray* all the way out to where a wall rose.

No — not a wall. A *fortress*. A wall that climbed so high it disappeared into the cloud cover, the stonework black and ancient and studded with lights that flickered like watching eyes. Every few hundred meters, a tower thrust up from the wall, bristling with what looked like artillery and what looked like *frost*.

And in the foreground, scattered across a patch of scorched earth like discarded dolls, were bodies.

*Soldiers.* The word came to him unbidden. The armor was wrong — too many angles, too many glowing runes — but the bodies were unmistakable. Men and women in ruined plate, half-eaten, half-grown-over with the same moss that was now drying on his cheek.

One of them, two meters to his left, was still *moving*.

Mu Chen's breath caught.

The soldier — *was* it a soldier?* — was on its back, armor cracked, helmet caved in. And from the cracks, *vines* were growing. Slow. Patient. Green leaves pushing up through the gaps in steel like the plant was trying to be born.

It turned its head toward him.

There was no face left. Just moss and a single eye, that one eye, glowing faintly amber.

It opened its mouth — a mouth that had no lower jaw — and made a sound like wind in dead reeds.

"*...s-s-ssss...ave...*"

Then it stopped moving.

Mu Chen sat very still for a long time.

---

When he finally stood up — legs shaking, hands shaking, dignity absolutely not shaking because he had none left — the first thing he did was check his pockets.

Phone: missing. Of course.

Wallet: missing. Of course.

The clothes on his back: the same Meishan-cousin's-wedding blazer and too-tight slacks he'd been wearing when he got on the scooter. Still had a sauce stain from the lunch order. Still had a tear in the left knee from where the curb kissed him.

But the *blazer*...

He looked down at his chest.

There was a book in his inside pocket.

It hadn't been there before.

It was old. Not *fake*-old like the prop books in those period dramas his mother watched. *Old* old. The leather cover was the color of dried blood, cracked and soft as skin, and the pages — yellow, hand-stitched — were so thin he could see the print bleeding through from the back.

No title on the cover.

No author.

He opened it.

On the inside cover, in faded black ink, in a handwriting that was somehow *both* crabbed and elegant:

**To my great-grandson, if the world has finally broken:**

**The soil remembers everything we forgot. Trust the carrot. Suspect the cauliflower. Never, ever plant a tomato you haven't personally insulted.**

**— Zeng Zu**

Mu Chen stared.

"Zeng Zu" was his great-grandfather's name. The one who died in '98, before Mu Chen was born, the one the family never talked about except in embarrassed whispers about "the old man's garden."

*How the hell is this in my pocket?*

Then the wind blew.

And the book *warmed* in his hands.

And the pages flipped, all by themselves, to a page that had no text on it until a second ago, until right *now*, where three lines of glowing blue characters were appearing like frost on glass:

---

**[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]**

**Host detected. Binding initiated.**

**Cultivation Module: AGRICULTURE (Class: Farmer) loading...**

**Subsystem: 万物生长手札 (Compendium of All Things That Grow) — ACTIVE**

---

Mu Chen dropped the book.

It didn't fall. It hovered, three inches above his palm, pages fluttering like a hummingbird's wings.

And then a second notification appeared:

---

**[QUEST LINE: THE FIRST SEED]**

**Objective: Plant one (1) viable seed in cultivable soil.**

**Reward: ???**

**Failure Condition: Death by starvation, exposure, or consumption.**

**Time Limit: 72 hours.**

**NOTE FROM THE COMPENDIUM:**

*Welcome back, little farmer. The soil has missed you. Please don't die in the first three days, that would be embarrassing for both of us.*

---

Mu Chen looked at the book.

The book looked at him. (It had no eyes. This was not a metaphor. The leather cover *faced* him. That was enough.)

"I'm going to die," he said to no one. "I'm going to die in seventy-two hours on a planet I can't even name, holding my great-grandfather's haunted cookbook, and the *book* is going to be *embarrassed*."

The book fluttered a page. The way a teacher might flip a textbook at a slow student.

*Get on with it.*

Mu Chen looked at the dead soldier. At the wall. At the gray sky.

He looked at his own hands — still smeared with that black-green moss-ichor, trembling.

He looked at the book.

"Fine," he said. "Fine. But I want it on record that I am *deeply* unhappy about this."

The book had no comment.

---

He walked.

For three hours, he walked.

Away from the bodies, because bodies meant *something* that made bodies, and he wanted no part of that. Toward the wall, because the wall was the only landmark that wasn't trying to eat him. Along a road that wasn't really a road, more a *track* of crushed earth, with the occasional rut that might once have been a wagon wheel.

The ground was wrong.

It looked like soil, but when he knelt down — once, near a small outcrop of rock that might once have been a hill — and pressed his palm to it, it felt *thin*. Like a sheet of cloth over a pit. Like the earth was *wearing* a costume of soil, and underneath was something else.

The system pulsed.

A new line appeared at the bottom of the hovering page:

---

**[COMPOST QUALITY: 0.3 / 10 — Pathetic]**

**[RECOMMENDATION: Find a water source. Even you can do that, and we mean that as a compliment.]**

---

Mu Chen gritted his teeth.

"Water," he said. "Yes. Even I can do that. Thank you. So helpful."

He walked another hour.

And then he found it.

A crack in the earth. Barely a hand's width. But *water* was seeping through, slow and clear and smelling of iron and something sweeter, something that reminded him — absurdly, irrelevantly, *painfully* — of the well behind his great-grandfather's house in Meishan.

He knelt. He cupped water in his hands. He drank.

It tasted of nothing. Clean. Cold.

He drank again. And again.

And then the system pulsed:

---

**[WATER SOURCE LOCATED — MARKED ON INTERNAL MAP]**

**[SOIL QUALITY ADJACENT TO WATER: 2.1 / 10 — Still pathetic, but workable]**

**[SEED INVENTORY: EMPTY]**

**[RECOMMENDATION: Look up.]**

---

Mu Chen looked up.

Above the crack in the earth, growing out of a crevice in a rock that was definitely, *definitely* not soil but was trying very hard to look like soil, was a plant.

It was tiny. Knee-high. With leaves that were brown at the edges and green in the middle, and a single, sad-looking fruit at the top.

It looked, to Mu Chen's untrained eye, like a tomato.

A *wretched* tomato.

But a tomato.

He reached for it.

The book *screamed*.

Not aloud. In his head. A sensation like a kettle coming to boil, like a teacher slapping a desk, like his grandmother's voice saying "don't touch that, you idiot child."

He jerked his hand back.

A new line pulsed red in the book:

---

**[⚠️ WARNING ⚠️]**

**[SPECIES DETECTED: BLOOD-VINE TOMATO — Class: Semi-Sentient / Semi-Toxic]**

**[DO NOT. WE REPEAT. DO NOT. CONSUME.]**

**[Subsidiary note from the Compendium: We are BEGGING you. Do not eat the tomato. The last farmer who ate the tomato grew a second mouth. It took us three years to train it out of him.]**

---

Mu Chen stared at the tomato.

The tomato — and he was *not* imagining this — seemed to *wobble* at him. Like it knew he'd been considering it.

"*I'm* not the one who grew a second mouth," Mu Chen said to the tomato.

The tomato wobbled harder.

He backed away from it.

He looked at the book.

The book had no comment, but the pages were trembling slightly, as if in residual fear.

---

This was his great-grandfather's legacy.

A haunted cookbook that thought he was stupid.

A planet that wanted to eat him.

A wall he couldn't see the top of.

And one seed.

One *seed* in the system inventory, blinking green like a Christmas light:

**[SEED INVENTORY: 1x Carrot (Common)]**

He didn't remember picking it up. He didn't remember it being there. But it was in the system, and the system was right about the water and right about the tomato and right about the fact that he was going to die in 72 hours if he didn't do something.

He knelt.

He dug a small hole in the soil next to the water source, using only his hands because he had no tools and no dignity and the universe, he was beginning to suspect, *also* had no tools and no dignity when it came to him.

He placed the carrot seed in the hole.

He covered it with earth.

He patted it down.

He looked at the book.

"Now what," he said.

The book pulsed.

A new line, gentle this time, almost *fond*:

---

**[SEED PLANTED.]**

**[CARROT — STAGE: SEED → GERMINATION]** (estimated: 8 hours)

**[SOIL QUALITY AT PLANTING SITE: 2.1 / 10 — Pathetic but sufficient]**

**[QUEST PROGRESS: 1/1 Carrot Planted]**

**[REWARD UNLOCKING IN: 7 hours, 58 minutes]**

**[SUBSIDIARY NOTE FROM THE COMPENDIUM:]**

*Good. That's one carrot. The soil thanks you. The book thanks you. We are, against all odds, proud of you. Now find shelter. The Night Tides come at moonrise, and they have not eaten a farmer in six weeks. Let's keep the streak alive, shall we?*

---

Mu Chen looked at the small patch of turned earth.

Then at the wall.

Then at the gray sky, where the sun — if it was a sun, it was the wrong color, a sort of sullen amber — was beginning to drop.

He had seven hours, fifty-eight minutes until his first reward.

He had maybe four hours until dark.

He had a *carrot seed* and a haunted cookbook and a *wretched tomato* that seemed to find him personally offensive.

He had no shelter. No weapon. No idea where he was. No idea who he was anymore.

He had, against all odds, a *plan*.

The plan was: don't die.

Mu Chen stood up. He brushed the soil off his knees. He tightened his blazer against the cold.

The book floated beside him like a small, judgmental parrot.

"Alright," he said to it, to the wall, to the gray sky, to whatever god or system or great-grandfather had seen fit to drop him here. "Alright. We do this your way. But I want it on record—"

He stopped. He sighed.

"—yeah. I know. You have no comment."

He started walking.

Toward the wall.

Toward the first of what he suspected would be many, many carrots.

---

In the last of the light, he saw her.

A figure, on the road ahead. Standing still. Watching him.

She was not wearing the moss-eaten armor of the dead soldiers. She wore a long gray coat, the hem stained dark, and she held a sword that was too long for the scabbard on her back, like she'd been given it as a punishment. Her hair was black and short and her face was—

Was—

Even at this distance, he could see that her face was *not happy to see him.*

Mu Chen stopped walking.

The book pulsed once, gently, with what he was beginning to recognize as *amusement*.

---

**[NPC DETECTED]**

**[NAME: Qin Shuang (秦霜)]**

**[CLASS: Wall-Commander, Hundred-Person Unit]**

**[AFFINITY: ???]**

**[RELATIONSHIP STATUS: Stranger (Hostile) ]**

**[Subsidiary note from the Compendium: Ah. Her. We've been waiting for you to meet her. Try not to be yourself. It will go better for everyone.]**

---

Mu Chen looked at the book.

"I'm going to die," he said, again, to no one, again.

But this time, for the first time since he'd opened his eyes in this cold, gray, body-strewn, haunted-tomato-having world, he was not entirely sure he was wrong.

---

**END CHAPTER 1**

---

## 写作笔记（主人后续修改用）

### Genre 公式（不侵权证据）
- ✅ 穿越 + 系统 + 升级 = 公共公式
- ✅ 种田 + 经营 = 公共公式
- ✅ 吐槽向第 1 人称 LitRPG = 公共公式
- ✅ 主角嘴碎 + 怕死 + 抠门 = 公共公式（与 BOC/DCC/HWFWM 都不同）
- ❌ 没有抄任何具体情节/角色名/对白

### Royal Road 发布需声明
- AI 辅助创作（草稿由 AI 生成，主人改 + native speaker 润色后发布）
- Royal Road 政策要求"AI Disclosure"

### 后续修改建议
1. **章节长度**：当前 ~3500 词，RR 主流 5K-8K，可以扩 Ch 2 起加到 5K
2. **节奏**：第 1 章偏慢热（铺垫穿越），Ch 2 起加快
3. **女主初遇**：Ch 1 末是伏笔（秦霜出现），Ch 2 应该是和秦霜的初遇对话
4. **状态面板**：保留 RR 风格，不要过多（元数据/技能/任务/属性 4 类即可）
5. **中文母语者写英文**：建议请 native speaker 改 1-2 遍，重点改 idiom 和俚语

### 文件结构
- Ch 1: 3500 词
- 设定 30 章 × 3500 词 = 105K 词 / 第 1 卷
- 7 卷共 735K 词（合中文约 200 万字）

### 下一步
1. 主人改 Ch 1（语言/细节）
2. 写 Ch 2（与秦霜初遇 + 第一次潮汐夜）
3. 找 native speaker 润色 1-2 遍
4. 建 Royal Road 账号发布

title: Chapter 2: The Wall Commander
content:
She looked at me the way my third-year math teacher had looked at me when I'd scored 37 on the midterm: like I was a biological inconvenience the universe had decided to inflict on everyone around me.

"I don't know how you got past the perimeter," she said. Her voice was flat. Not angry. Not curious. *Flat*, like the surface of a frozen lake right before you fell through it. "But you're going back."

"I'm sorry," I said, and the words came out wrong because I was still shaking from everything — the bodies, the moss-soldier, the haunted tomato, the *carrot seed* — and my voice cracked in the middle like a twelve-year-old's. "I'm sorry, I don't — I don't know where I am. I woke up and there were dead people and *vines* growing out of their faces and I walked toward the wall because it was the only thing that wasn't dead, and—"

"You're talking too much."

"Yes. Sorry."

She took a step closer.

I took a step back.

The book floated behind me like a nervous relative at a police station. I could feel it pulsing, occasionally, little warmth pulses against my shoulder blade, as if it was sending a message it couldn't quite articulate.

She noticed the book.

Her hand went to her sword.

"Hold," she said. Not to me. To the book.

The book did not hold. It fluttered, defensively, like a well-trained dog that had just been shouted at.

Her eyes narrowed.

"What is that."

"I have absolutely no idea," I said. Which was true. "It was in my pocket when I woke up. My great-grandfather's book. I think. His name was Zeng Zu. I think. I never met him, he died before I was born, but the book says he's my great-grandfather, and also that the soil remembers things, and also that I should trust the carrot and suspect the cauliflower, which I think is insane advice, frankly, and—"

"*Stop talking.*"

I stopped talking.

She stared at me for a long moment. Then she looked at the book. Then she looked back at me.

"Are you a cultivator?"

"I'm a delivery driver," I said. "Was. Was a delivery driver. On Earth. Chengdu. I delivered food. Noodles, mostly. Sometimes hot pot. The hot pot deliveries were a nightmare because the soup would leak and—"

"*Mu Chen.*"

My name. She said my name. I hadn't told her my name.

The book pulsed, warm, and a new line appeared on the hovering page:

---

**[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]**

**You have been identified.**

**Identity confirmed: Mu Chen, citizen of Earth (Deceased), current status: Displaced Person.**

**Compendium note: Qin Shuang, Hundred-Person Commander of the Wall Guard, Third Northern District. She has killed forty-seven Abyss creatures personally. She has a scar on her left shoulder blade from a Vine Stalker. She is not currently eating, but she is considering eating you as a substitute.*

---

I stared at the new notification.

Then I stared at her.

"Forty-seven," I said, without meaning to.

"What?"

"Nothing. Sorry. Voice in my head. Long story."

She took another step. I took another step back. We were doing this dance now, the two-step, me retreating and her advancing, like some kind of very slow, very uncomfortable waltz.

"The Night Tides start in ninety minutes," she said. "You will come with me. You will be processed at the Wall checkpoint. If you are not an Abyss agent — and you *feel* like a very stupid, very lost, very *unlucky* person, which is usually a good sign against agents — you will be assigned temporary housing inside the wall."

"Inside the wall," I repeated.

"Yes."

"So I'd be... *inside* the wall? Safe from the Night Tides?"

Her jaw tightened. Just slightly. But I caught it.

"There is no 'safe,'" she said. "Inside the wall is *safer*. Outside the wall is *dead*." She paused. "Where you were. On that road. That's outside the wall. That's the Perimeter Zone. No civilians are supposed to be there."

"I'm not a civilian. I'm a delivery driver."

"Civilian."

"Fine. Civilian."

"And yet." She gestured at me. At the book. At the fact that I was standing here, talking, instead of being eaten or moss-covered or *gone*. "You survived three hours outside. Unarmed. Unarmored. In a *blazer*."

I looked down at the blazer. My cousin's wedding blazer. The one with the sauce stain. The one I had been wearing when a truck turned me into a meat projectile on a Chengdu street corner.

"It's a good blazer," I said.

"It has sauce on it."

"It's a *versatile* blazer."

Something happened to her face. A twitch. Tiny. Micro. The kind of twitch that might have been suppressed laughter if Qin Shuang had ever learned how to laugh.

But she crushed it immediately. Her expression went back to granite.

"Come," she said, and turned and started walking toward the wall.

I stood there.

"Come *now*," she said, without turning around.

"I have a carrot," I said.

She stopped.

She turned.

"*What?*"

"A carrot. I planted it. It's—" I pointed back toward where I'd come from, where the water seep was, where the sad little patch of turned earth was hopefully not being eaten by anything. "—it's about two kilometers back. It's germinating. I have maybe seven hours until it matures and I get a reward and I *cannot leave it*."

She stared at me.

"The Night Tides will eat it," she said.

"Probably."

"And you."

"Also probably."

"So you're choosing a *carrot* over your *life*."

"I'm choosing to check on my *investment* before abandoning it, which is different. I'm a delivery driver. I always completed the delivery before I quit."

"I don't know what that means."

"Neither do I," I admitted. "But I'm going back to the carrot."

She stood there. She was maybe five meters away from me. Her hand was on her sword. She was taller than me by at least ten centimeters, and she carried herself like a weapon, which I suppose she was.

The book pulsed. A new notification:

---

**[NPC DETECTED]**

**[Qin Shuang — WALL-COMMANDER, HUNDRED-PERSON]**

**[Current mood: Confused. Irritated. Also, very slightly, intrigued.]**

**[Relationship status: Stranger (Hostile → ???)]**

**[Compendium note: She's never met anyone who prioritized a carrot over fleeing from her. This is new for her. She doesn't know what to do with new. Be patient.]**

---

I read the notification.

I looked at Qin Shuang.

She was still standing there, hand on sword, face unreadable.

"Two kilometers," she said.

"What?"

"You have two kilometers. I'll walk with you. We check the carrot. Then you come inside the wall. Non-negotiable." She paused. "If the carrot is dead, or eaten, or planted in Abyss soil, you come *immediately*. Do you understand?"

"Yes," I said. "Thank you."

"Don't thank me. Move."

We walked.

---

The walk back was... strange.

Qin Shuang moved through the Perimeter Zone like she had memorized every rock, every depression in the earth, every shadow. She walked two meters to my left — never directly beside me, never behind me — and her eyes never stopped scanning. The sword was drawn now, held low, the blade dark and faintly iridescent.

The book floated between us, which seemed to bother her, but she'd stopped threatening it, which I took as progress.

"What is that?" she asked, not looking at me.

"The book?"

"Yes."

"Like I said, my great-grandfather's."

"Your great-grandfather was outside the wall?"

"I don't know. I don't know anything about him except his name and the fact that he apparently wanted me to trust carrots and be suspicious of cauliflower. My family never talked about him. My grandmother cried every time he was mentioned, so eventually we just... didn't mention him."

"That's unusual."

"He's unusual. He left me a haunted farming book. He left me absolutely zero explanation. And he apparently thought I would be better off on a monster-infested hellscape than on a Chengdu street corner delivering noodles." I kicked a pebble. It skittered across the gray earth and disappeared into a crack. "Honestly? He might have had a point about the noodles."

She didn't laugh. But she didn't tell me to shut up either.

"The book called you a cultivator," she said. "Outside the wall, only cultivators can make things grow. The Abyss kills ordinary soil. That's why there are no farms out here — not real ones. Just the wall, and the soldiers, and the things that come out at night."

"There are farms," I said. "I saw — when I was walking — I saw plots. Cleared patches. Near the water sources."

She went very still.

"You saw *farms*?"

"Yes?"

"Deserted?"

"Some of them. Others had people. I didn't approach. I was — I was busy trying not to die."

"The cultivators," she said, quietly. "They're the ones who farm outside the wall. They work for the Wall Guard — officially, as 'Perimeter Support.' They grow food for the soldiers, medicines for the wounded, materials for the fortifications." She paused. "It's a six-month assignment. Most of them don't last three."

"Why?"

"Because the Night Tides are bad. Because the soil is sick. Because growing things outside the wall is—" She stopped. "—it's not farming. It's war. With the earth."

The book pulsed.

---

**[QUEST LINE: THE FIRST SEED — UPDATE]**

**Status: IN PROGRESS**

**Time remaining: 7 hours, 22 minutes**

**Carrot status: GERMINATION → SPROUT (67%)**

**Threat level at site: UNKNOWN**

**Compendium note: Your carrot is currently fine. Possibly the only thing on this planet that is fine. The Night Tides haven't reached the water seep yet. Don't jinx it.*

---

I checked the notification and felt a small, stupid surge of relief. My carrot was okay.

The book pulsed again, and a new line appeared at the bottom:

---

**[NEW REGISTRY AVAILABLE]**

**Name: Mu Chen**

**Status: Unregistered Perimeter Cultivator (Probationary)**

**Assignment Zone: Unassigned**

**Pending action: Wall checkpoint processing (REQUIRED)**

**Perks of registration: Access to Wall supply depot, legal protection, monthly ration allotment.**

**Drawbacks of registration: You belong to the Wall. Your time, your crops, your *life* belong to the Wall Guard. Qin Shuang will be your commanding officer. She will order you to do things like "stand in front of the monster" and "plant explosives in the ground." You will comply. This is the deal.*

**Alternative: Refuse registration. Operate as an independent cultivator. Die in 97% of cases. Die badly in the other 3%.*

---

I read this very carefully.

"You look worried," Qin Shuang said.

"I'm always worried," I said. "It's my resting state. Like how some people's resting state is 'seems fine' or 'probably chill,' mine is 'actively catastrophizing.'"

She didn't ask what "catastrophizing" meant. She was learning.

"The Perimeter Cultivator program," she said. "That's what you'll be registered as. Temporary assignment, three months to start, renewable based on performance. You get a plot near the wall, access to basic seeds and tools, and a percentage of what you grow."

"A *percentage*?"

"Twenty percent of total yield. Eighty percent goes to the Wall Guard." She glanced at me. "It's better than zero percent. Which is what you get if you're not registered."

"And if I don't want to be registered?"

She stopped walking.

We'd reached the water seep. I could see the small patch of earth I'd turned, the tiny green sprout barely visible above the soil. It looked impossibly small. Impossibly fragile. Like the universe had given me one tiny thing and then set up elaborate mechanisms to destroy it.

She was looking at the sprout.

"You're not registered," she said. "You're outside the wall. You have no legal status. No protection. No ration card. No access to the supply depot. If you get hurt, there's no healer. If your farm gets raided, there's no justice." She turned to face me. "You also have no *assignment*. The Wall Guard doesn't know you exist. Which means no one will come if you're attacked. No one will know if you die."

"So my options are: join the Wall Guard, or die alone in a field."

"Your options are: join the Wall Guard and live long enough to complain about the twenty-eighty split, or try to farm alone in the Perimeter Zone and die in ninety-seven percent of cases."

"Ninety-seven percent."

"It's a well-documented statistic."

"I feel like ninety-seven percent should have more decimal places."

"It does. We rounded."

I looked at the sprout. My sprout. My stupid, tiny, improbably hopeful sprout.

I looked at the wall, visible now as a massive dark line on the horizon, its lights flickering like eyes.

I looked at Qin Shuang.

Her face told me nothing. She was a stone. She was a wall. She was the personification of the wall itself — immovable, unsympathetic, and very, very good at killing things that tried to get past her.

"Twenty percent," I said.

"What?"

"Your split. Twenty percent of yield goes to the cultivator. That's—"

"That's the standard rate."

"The *standard* rate. For people who have been doing this for years, who have tools, seeds, experience, and who know what the hell they're doing. I'm a *delivery driver*. I have a blazer with sauce on it and a haunted book and one carrot seed that probably won't survive the night."

"Then your twenty percent of zero is zero," she said. Flat. Matter-of-fact. Not cruel, exactly. Just honest. The kind of honest that was its own form of violence.

I stood there.

The book pulsed.

And a new notification appeared, the text glowing soft blue:

---

**[QUEST LINE: WALL REGISTRATION]**

**Objective: Submit to registration processing at Wall checkpoint.**

**Alternative Objective: REFUSE registration. Operate as independent cultivator.**

**Compendium note: We recommend registration. We understand that you find the twenty-eighty split... creatively disagreeable. But consider: you currently have zero seeds, zero tools, zero knowledge, zero allies, and a compost quality of 0.3 out of ten. The Wall Guard is not your friend. But they are your *infrastructure*. Every great farm needs infrastructure. Even if the infrastructure sometimes treats you like a tax obligation.*

---

I read the notification. I read it twice.

"You have that look," Qin Shuang said.

"What look?"

"The look of someone talking to their book. Stop it. It's unsettling."

"It's *my* book."

"It's a haunted artifact from outside the known territories. I should confiscate it."

"Please don't."

"Then stop making faces at the air."

I made a face at her instead. A small one. Petty. Very me.

She almost smiled.

It was so fast I might have imagined it. A twitch at the corner of her mouth. There and gone. Like a fish surfacing in dark water.

But I saw it.

"I'll register," I said.

"Good."

"But I want thirty percent."

"You want—"

"Thirty percent. I'm an *unknown quantity*. Unregistered cultivator with a system artifact. That makes me valuable. Valuable things get better terms." I paused. "Also I don't know what I'm doing and I will probably kill myself within the first week, which means you should invest in keeping me alive long enough to be useful."

She stared at me.

I stared back.

The book pulsed what I could only describe as *approval*.

"Twenty-five," she said.

"Thirty."

"Twenty-five. Final offer. And I'll personally supervise your first planting."

"Yourself?"

"I'm the one who found you. That makes you my responsibility until you're registered and assigned." She said it like it tasted bad. Like "responsibility" was a vegetable she'd been forced to eat. "Twenty-five percent. Take it or try to survive alone."

"Twenty-five percent," I said. "With your supervision."

"Reluctantly."

"Reluctantly accepted."

She turned and started walking toward the wall.

I looked at my sprout one more time. It was still there. Still green. Still impossibly small against the gray of everything else.

"Come *on*," she called.

"I'm saying goodbye to my carrot."

"It's a plant."

"It's my *investment*."

I crouched beside the sprout. I put my hand near it — not touching, I wasn't stupid, not after the blood-vine tomato — just near it. A small gesture. A small promise.

"Don't die," I told it. "I'm going to be back. I have a registration to do and a twenty-five percent split to negotiate and apparently a whole wall full of people who are going to treat me like a tax obligation, but I will *be back*."

The sprout didn't respond. Obviously. It was a sprout.

But the book pulsed, warm, and a new line appeared:

---

**[COMPANION BOND INITIATED: CARROT SPROUT]**

**Bond strength: 1%**

**Note: Your carrot cannot hear you. But it can feel you. And it is, in the only way a plant can be, grateful. The soil is also grateful. And so, against all odds and expectations, are we.*

---

I stood up.

The book floated beside me, glowing softly.

And Qin Shuang, thirty meters ahead, stopped and turned and looked at me with an expression that I would later learn to recognize as *long-suffering patience* — the look she gave every person who made her job harder than it needed to be.

I walked toward her.

Behind me, the sprout stood alone in the gray earth, impossibly green, impossibly small.

I would protect it.

Or I would die trying.

Which, given my track record, was approximately equally likely.

---

**[STATUS UPDATE]**

**Compost: 0.3 / 10**

**Seeds: 1x Carrot (Planted)**

**Active quest: THE FIRST SEED — 7h 14m remaining**

**Pending quest: WALL REGISTRATION — In progress**

**Reputation: Qin Shuang (Stranger — Hostile)**

**Relationship: Qin Shuang — Reluctant Acquisition**

**Companion: Carrot Sprout (Bond: 1%)**

**Compendium note: Qin Shuang walked you back instead of arresting you. She is not a patient woman. She does not do reluctant. She does not do thirty percent splits. Something about you made her do all three. We don't know what. But we're watching with great interest. Also, your carrot is alone in the dark. The Night Tides are in four hours. Try not to let this haunt you. (It will haunt you.)*

---

**END CHAPTER 2**

---

## 写作笔记

### 状态变化
- Compost: 0.3 (不变)
- 新增：COMPANION BOND — Carrot Sprout 1%
- 关系：Qin Shuang Hostile → Reluctant Acquisition（她愿意给30%谈判机会，说明有兴趣）
- 新机制预告：Wall Guard 征兵系统（20-80 split / 25-75 split）

### 钩子说明
- 钩子1：Carrot sprout 独自留在 Perimeter Zone，Night Tides 四小时后到达
- 钩子2：秦霜的"almost smiled"暗示软化
- 钩子3：Wall Guard 征兵条款（主角被绑定 vs 独立农夫的97%死亡率）

### Genre 安全
- ✅ 吐槽第1人称 LitRPG（公共公式）
- ✅ 谈判桥段（公共公式）
- ✅ 植物-人类 bond（公共公式）
- ❌ 无抄任何具体作品

title: Chapter 4: First Night Tide
content:
# The Abyss Rancher: How I Saved the World by Farming
## Chapter 4: First Night Tide

---

The Perimeter Zone at dusk was a different animal than the Perimeter Zone at noon.

It wasn't just darker. It was *hungry*. The gray sky had taken on a bruised quality, purple-black at the edges, and the air tasted different — metallic, like blood and old pennies. The ground beneath my feet seemed to breathe. Not metaphorically. *Literally*. Subtle pulses, like something underneath was inhaling.

Qin Shuang moved like a ghost. Not the slow, deliberate movements of a soldier on patrol, but the fluid, silent motion of something that had learned to move through darkness without disturbing it. I sounded like a small earthquake behind her. Every step I took seemed to echo for a kilometer.

"Quiet," she said.

"I'm trying."

"Try harder."

"I'm being as quiet as a man in dress shoes on gravel can be."

"You're not wearing dress shoes. You're wearing—" She glanced back at my feet. "—shoes."

"Shoes that came from Earth. Shoes that have never been tested against an Abyss-zone Night Tide. Shoes that are, frankly, handling the situation worse than I am, and I just learned that my planet is trying to kill me."

She didn't tell me to shut up. She didn't slow down. She just kept moving, two meters ahead, sword drawn, eyes scanning.

The book floated between us, glowing faintly. It pulsed every few seconds, like a heartbeat, and every pulse brought a new notification:

---

**[LOCATION: PERIMETER ZONE — OUTER RING]**

**Distance to sprout: 1.7 km**

**Night Tide ETA: 1 hour, 48 minutes**

**Ambient threat level: ELEVATED**

**Compendium note: You are walking into a Night Tide. This is still a terrible idea. We want that on record. Also, something ahead just moved. Something large. We don't know what it is yet. Qin Shuang has noticed it too. She hasn't drawn her sword to a higher readiness position because that would make a sound, and she is trying very hard not to make a sound. This is a situation.*

---

I read the notification. I looked at Qin Shuang.

Her sword was still drawn at the same position. Low. Ready. But her shoulders had changed. Subtle tension. A shift in weight. She was getting ready for something.

"What is it?" I whispered.

"Don't know."

"What do you mean you don't know?"

"I mean I don't know. I know most of the things in the Perimeter Zone. This one is new. Or hiding."

"Hiding from what?"

"From me. From anyone. These things don't usually come out until moonrise." She paused. "Whatever's circling your carrot, it's early."

The book pulsed.

---

**[⚠️ WARNING ⚠️]**

**[THREAT IDENTIFIED: HOLLOW STALKER — Class: Ambush Predator]**

**Description: Tall, thin, vaguely humanoid. Uses bioluminescent lure to attract prey. Does not breathe. Does not have a heart. Does have teeth. Approximately 2.5 meters tall. Approximately 200 kilograms. Approximately very interested in you.*

**Recommendation: Do not approach. Do not look at the lure. Do not make eye contact. Qin Shuang can probably kill it. Probably. We are sixty percent sure. (We are being optimistic.)*

---

I stopped walking.

"What?"

"Keep moving."

"Something's out there. The book says—"

"I know what's out there." She didn't look back. "A Hollow Stalker. Juvenile, probably. They hunt alone until they mature. This one is circling something — your sprout, most likely. The lure would attract it."

"The *lure*?"

"The bioluminescent light. Hollow Stalkers produce it — a soft blue glow, like a lantern in the dark. Prey sees it, thinks it's a traveler, a survivor, a light from the wall. They walk toward it. They get close." She paused. "They don't walk away."

"What does the lure look like?"

"Blue. Soft. Warm-looking." A pause. "Exactly like the glow of your book."

I looked at the book.

The book looked at me.

We both looked at Qin Shuang.

"I've been using this book as a flashlight," I said.

"Yes."

"In the dark."

"Yes."

"And you didn't say anything."

"You didn't ask."

We stared at each other in the dark.

"Put the book away," she said.

"I can't. It floats."

"Then turn it off."

"I don't know how. It has a mind of its own."

The book pulsed. Defensively. The way a pet might if you'd accused it of something it absolutely had done.

---

**[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]**

**Clarification: The Compendium cannot be "turned off." It is a magical artifact, not a smartphone. (We understand you wish it were a smartphone. We wish we were a smartphone. This would make everything simpler.) However, we can reduce the glow. Brace yourself. It will feel strange.*

---

The book dimmed. Not off — just dimmer. The blue glow faded to a faint shimmer, barely visible, like moonlight on water.

I felt a strange sensation. Not pain. More like... pressure. Like the air around me had thickened.

"Don't freak out," Qin Shuang said.

"I'm not freaking out."

"Your eyes are glowing."

"My eyes are *what*?"

"The system artifact. When you bonded with it, it left a trace marker. It's visible in darkness. Not to most things." She paused. "But Hollow Stalkers can see it. They hunt by following system glows. It's how they find cultivators."

"So the book is..."

"A beacon. Yes. A glowing dinner bell for the things that eat cultivators."

I looked at the book. The book, dimmed but still faintly visible, pulsed with what I could only interpret as embarrassment.

"My great-grandfather," I said slowly, "left me a beacon that attracts monsters."

"Your great-grandfather," Qin Shuang said, "was either a genius or insane. Possibly both. The cultivators who survived longest were the ones who learned to suppress the glow. Or kill whatever was following it before it killed them."

"Can we do the second one?"

"Maybe."

"What's the success rate for 'maybe'?"

"Sixty percent. Like the book said."

"That's not very high."

"Do you have a better idea?"

I didn't.

---

We moved.

The Hollow Stalker was ahead. Maybe fifty meters. Maybe less. I could see it now — or rather, I could see the *shape* of it, against the darkening sky. Tall. Thin. Arms that were too long. Legs that bent backward. Like someone had drawn a person from memory, but the memory was of something that had only heard descriptions of people.

It wasn't moving.

It was just... standing there.

Waiting.

And from its chest — where a chest would be, if it were a person — a soft blue light pulsed. Gentle. Warm. Like a lantern on a doorstep. Like safety. Like home.

My feet almost moved toward it.

"Stop," Qin Shuang whispered.

I stopped.

"The lure," she said. "It's working. Don't look at it. Look at me."

I looked at her. Her face was barely visible in the dim light. Hard lines. Hard jaw. Eyes that reflected the blue glow behind us like chips of obsidian.

"The sprout," I said. "It's between us and the stalker."

"Yes."

"How do we—"

"We're not going around. We're going through." She adjusted her grip on her sword. "I go first. You stay behind me. If it charges, you run. Back to the wall. Don't look back."

"And the sprout?"

"I'll handle the sprout. You handle not dying."

"That's a very asymmetric distribution of tasks."

"That's a battle plan."

The book pulsed:

---

**[QUEST LINE UPDATE: THE FIRST SEED — RESCUE MISSION]**

**Objective: Reach carrot sprout.**

**Obstacle: Hollow Stalker (Juvenile)**

**Support: Qin Shuang (sixty percent success rate)**

**Compendium note: You are about to watch Qin Shuang fight a monster. She is very good at fighting monsters. She is also the reason most of you are still alive. Please do not die in the next thirty seconds. It would be embarrassing for everyone. (Especially the book. We take these things personally.)*

---

She moved.

It was not a sprint. It was something else. Something faster. Something that didn't obey the normal rules of momentum and friction and the human body's limitations. She covered the fifty meters between us and the Hollow Stalker in what felt like a single breath.

The sword came up.

The Hollow Stalker *screamed*.

It was not a human scream. It was a sound like tearing metal, like glass breaking, like every sound that had ever hurt your ears compressed into one moment of pure auditory violence. I clamped my hands over my ears and dropped to one knee.

Through the blur of pain and sound, I saw Qin Shuang hit the Hollow Stalker like a freight train made of steel and fury.

She didn't slash. She didn't hack. She *cut*. Precise. Economical. Like she was disassembling it rather than fighting it. Each stroke had a purpose. Each movement was efficient. No wasted energy. No wasted motion.

The Hollow Stalker thrashed. Its long arms reached for her. She stepped inside its reach — inside, where those long arms couldn't find her — and drove her blade through what would have been its heart.

The blue light went out.

The creature fell.

Qin Shuang stood over it for a moment, breathing hard. Her sword was covered in something black and viscous. Not blood. Something else. Something that smoked faintly when it touched the ground.

She turned to me.

"Are you dead?"

"No," I said, ears still ringing. "No, I'm not dead. I have ringing in my ears, but I'm not dead."

"Good. Then get up. We have a carrot to save."

---

The sprout was fine.

It was sitting in its little patch of turned earth, two kilometers from the wall, surrounded by the corpses of the Perimeter Zone, thirty meters from where a Hollow Stalker had been preparing to eat it, and it was *fine*.

More than fine.

When I knelt beside it, the book pulsed, warm, and a new notification appeared:

---

**[QUEST LINE: THE FIRST SEED — COMPLETE]**

**Status: HARVEST READY**

**Carrot maturity: 100%**

**Quality assessment: COMMON — but viable. Not spectacular. Not impressive. But alive. That counts for something.*

**Reward unlocking: NOW**

---

A soft glow surrounded the sprout. Then it dimmed. Then the sprout... changed. Not dramatically — it was still a carrot — but the leaves were fuller, the stem thicker, and the orange root beneath the soil seemed to pulse faintly with light.

The book opened to a new page. Not the system notifications — something else. A page with actual text. Handwritten text, in that same crabbed, elegant script from the inside cover:

**"First harvest. Mine. The best carrot you will ever grow. Eat it and gain nothing but satisfaction. Share it, and gain something better. Trust this: the first thing you grow sets the tone for everything that follows. Plant selfishness, reap selfishness. Plant generosity, reap generosity. Plant a damn good carrot, and maybe — maybe — the soil will remember you fondly when you're gone.**

**Good luck, little farmer. The Night Tides are starting. Run."**

The text faded.

The sky behind the wall had gone fully dark.

And in the distance, past the wall, past the city, past everything — I could hear it.

The Night Tide.

Not a wave. Not a tide of water. A tide of *sound*. Like ten thousand voices screaming in a language that predated language. Like the earth itself was roaring.

"Run," Qin Shuang said.

We ran.

---

The wall gates were closing when we reached them.

Not slowly. Not ceremonially. The gates were *slamming* — great slabs of black stone, rune-covered, groaning as they met in the middle. Qin Shuang grabbed my arm and pulled me through the narrowing gap.

I felt the wind of it on my back. The gates met behind us with a sound like a tomb sealing.

We were inside.

I was alive.

The book pulsed one final notification:

---

**[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]**

**COMPOST QUALITY UPDATED: 0.3 → 0.6**

**Explanation: First successful harvest. The soil has been touched by your effort. The soil remembers. This is a good thing. Compost is the foundation of everything. Zero compost means dead soil. High compost means living soil. Your soil is slightly less dead than it was. This is a triumph.*

**New item added to inventory: 1x Carrot (Common, Harvested)**

**Compendium note: You saved your carrot. You watched Qin Shuang kill a Hollow Stalker. You made it back before the Night Tides hit. This is, by the standards of your first full day in this world, a spectacular success. Your great-grandfather would be proud. (He is, probably. Somewhere. In whatever afterlife farmers go to.)*

---

I stood in the courtyard inside the wall, breathing hard, alive, holding a carrot I had grown with my own hands from a seed I had planted four hours ago.

It was a small carrot.

It was the most beautiful thing I had ever seen.

Qin Shuang was looking at me.

"You have dirt on your face," she said.

"I have dirt on my everything."

"I can see that."

"The Night Tides," I said, between breaths. "What are they? What was that sound?"

"Every night. Midnight to four AM. The Abyss pushes. Everything that lives in the dark comes to the surface. They hit the outer wall. They try to get through. Most of them fail." She paused. "Most of them."

"And the ones that don't?"

"We call them 'bad nights.'"

She turned and started walking toward the inner barracks.

"Edge Farm 47," she said, without looking back. "First thing tomorrow. You're planting that carrot. The one in your hand. You're going to plant it in cursed soil and see what happens."

"What if it doesn't grow?"

"Then we'll know the curse is stronger than you."

"And if it does?"

She stopped.

She turned.

For just a moment — a fraction of a second — the ghost of something crossed her face. Not quite a smile. Not quite hope. Something in between.

"Then we'll know you're worth keeping alive."

She walked away.

I stood there, holding my carrot, listening to the Night Tides roar outside the wall.

Tomorrow, I would plant it.

Tomorrow, I would find out if I was worth keeping alive.

Tonight, I would eat something. Sleep somewhere. And try very hard not to think about the sound of ten thousand screaming voices outside my window.

---

**[STATUS UPDATE]**

**Compost: 0.6 / 10 (+0.3)**

**Seeds: 1x Carrot (Harvested, Common)**

**Active quest: PLANT THE FIRST HARVEST — Tomorrow**

**Skills unlocked: None yet. Keep farming.**

**Reputation: Qin Shuang — Acolyte of the Stupid → ???**

**Relationship: Qin Shuang — Respect (grudging)**

**Companion: Carrot (Bond: 5%, HARVESTED)**

**Compendium note: You survived your first Night Tide. You saved your first harvest. You watched your first monster die. And you did not, for once, die yourself. Well done, Mu Chen. The soil is proud. We are proud. Qin Shuang is... processing. Give her time. She's never had a cultivator who ran *toward* a monster before. She doesn't know what to do with you yet. Neither do we. But we are excited to find out.*

---

**END CHAPTER 4**

---

## 写作笔记

### 状态变化
- Compost: 0.3 → 0.6 (+0.3，首次收获加成)
- 种子：胡萝卜 1→收获状态（库存+1）
- 关系：Qin Shuang → Respect (grudging)
- 新机制预告：Hollow Stalker（用 system glow 捕猎）
- 新钩子：Edge Farm 47 的诅咒土壤能不能种活胡萝卜？

### 钩子说明
- 钩子1：Night Tides 声音的恐怖描写（悬念）
- 钩子2：秦霜说"bad nights"（什么情况下会失败？）
- 钩子3：诅咒土壤种胡萝卜（核心悬念）

### Genre 安全
- ✅ 战斗场景（Qin Shuang 单挑 Hollow Stalker，60%成功率）
- ✅ 第一次收获（公共公式）
- ✅ 农夫系统：compost 机制
- ❌ 无抄任何具体作品

title: Chapter 11: The Thorn Wall
content:
# The Abyss Rancher: How I Saved the World by Farming
## Chapter 11: The Thorn Wall

---

The Thorn Seeds arrived on Day 9.

Lao Wang brought them back from his supply run, wrapped in oiled cloth, in a pouch that smelled faintly of copper and old leather. He'd traded with a caravan merchant who'd come in from the eastern settlements — the same settlements that had lost their supply caravan to the Tide surge, the settlements that were so desperate for fresh vegetables they'd started sending scouts toward every farm on the perimeter.

"Merchant said these are rare," Lao Wang said, handing me the pouch. "Doesn't sell them to just anyone. I told him I worked for a cultivator who grew a radish that tastes like hope, and he practically gave them away."

"Practically?"

"Only cost me two radishes."

"I haven't harvested any radishes since yesterday."

"You will. You always do."

I opened the pouch.

Inside: five seeds. Small. Brown. Unremarkable. If I'd found them on the ground, I'd have assumed they were dirt.

The book woke up immediately.

---

**[NEW SEED IDENTIFIED: THORN (Defensive Barrier Crop)]**

**Species: *Spina securus***

**Class: Defensive — Structural**

**Properties:**

- **Rapid growth**: Thorn seeds germinate within 24 hours of planting
- **Structural expansion**: Mature Thorn plants form interlocking barrier networks
- **Active defense**: Thorns regenerate damage within 2 hours; entangle hostile entities within radius
- **Non-lethal to friendly**: Thorn barriers do not affect cultivators or bonded companions

**Growth requirements: Compost 1.0+, Soil quality 1.5+, full sun exposure**

**Growth cycle: 5 days (seedling → barrier-ready → mature)**

**Warning: Thorn barriers are permanent once established. Removal requires burning the entire root system. Plan your layout carefully.**

**Compendium note: Thorn. The humble guardian of the Perimeter Zone. Every serious cultivator plants Thorn eventually. It's not glamorous. It's not exciting. It's a wall made of spikes. But when the Night Tides come, and the entities try to pour through your gate, and the soldiers are overwhelmed — the Thorn is what stands between them and your crops. The Thorn is what says: no further. The Thorn is agriculture's answer to the question: what do you do when everything wants to eat you? You grow a hedge of death and dare them to cross it.*

---

"How much did you spend on these?" I asked Lao Wang.

"Two radishes. Plus I told him about the green taste."

"So... effectively free?"

"Effectively free is the best price."

Little Seven was on her post, watching. She'd been watching since dawn — not because anything had happened, but because Little Seven watched. It was what she did. It was all she did, as far as I could tell, between eating and sleeping and occasionally shooting things.

"What are those?" she asked.

"Thorn seeds."

"What do they do?"

"They grow into walls."

"Wall walls?"

"Wall walls. Thorn walls. Spiky walls. Walls made of—"

"I understand what a wall is." She tilted her head. "Why do you need walls? You have us."

"I have you and Lao Wang. You can't watch the farm 24 hours a day."

"We take shifts."

"Even with shifts, you can't physically stop every entity that tries to get in. Lao Wang can fight maybe three at once. You can shoot maybe five before they close the distance. What happens when twenty come at once?"

Little Seven was quiet.

"Last night," she said finally, "we had twelve contact events."

"And we barely held them."

"Barely is still holding."

"Barely is not a long-term strategy."

Lao Wang came over. "He's right. Last night was light. Category 2. When the big Tides come — Category 4, Category 5 — we can't hold the perimeter with swords and arrows. We need—" He gestured at the farm. "—something else."

"Thorns," I said.

"Thorns."

---

I spent the morning planning the layout.

The book helped. It showed me a map of Edge Farm 47 — all four plots, the gate, the boundary markers, the perimeter — and overlaid it with potential Thorn barrier positions.

---

**[THORN LAYOUT — RECOMMENDED CONFIGURATION]**

**Option A: Perimeter Fence**
- Thorn barrier along the entire outer boundary
- Pros: Maximum coverage, protects all plots
- Cons: Expensive (requires 4+ Thorn Seeds), takes 5 days to fully grow
- Defense rating: HIGH

**Option B: Gate Guard**
- Thorn barrier focused on the entrance
- Pros: Protects main access point, uses fewer seeds
- Cons: Leaves plots 3 and 4 exposed
- Defense rating: MEDIUM

**Option C: Layered Defense**
- Thorn barrier at the perimeter + secondary barrier around Plot 1 (Prima's location)
- Pros: Protects most valuable asset (Prima), creates kill zones
- Cons: Complex layout, requires precise timing
- Defense rating: VERY HIGH

**Book recommendation: Option C**

**Rationale: The Prima is the heart of your farm. Everything grows because of it. If the Prima is destroyed, your farm dies. Protecting it should be your highest priority. A Thorn barrier around Plot 1 — combined with the Prima's existing Abyss Repulsion field — would create a nearly impenetrable defensive core.**

**Compendium note: Also, Option C is what your great-grandfather did. His farm had three layers of Thorn barriers by the end of his first year. He used to say: "Build your walls low and your roots deep. The things that come over the wall will die on the thorns. The things that come under the wall will starve waiting for roots." This is sound agricultural wisdom and also sound military wisdom. The two are more similar than most people realize.*

---

I chose Option C.

I planted three Thorn seeds.

Seed One: along the southern edge of Plot 1, forming a semicircle around the Prima.

Seed Two: at the gate entrance, blocking the main access point.

Seed Three: along the western edge of Plot 4, where the last Vine Stalker had tried to enter.

The fourth and fifth seeds I held in reserve. The book recommended keeping them — Thorn barriers could be extended, reinforced, or redirected as the farm grew.

---

**[THORN SEEDS — PLANTED]**

**Seed 1: Plot 1 — Southern Barrier (germinating)**

**Seed 2: Gate — Entrance Guard (germinating)**

**Seed 3: Plot 4 — Western Perimeter (germinating)**

**Growth status: GERMINATION — 24 hours to seedling stage**

**Full barrier formation: Day 14 (5 days from planting)**

**Compost: 1.5 / 10 (sufficient for Thorn growth)**

**Soil quality: 2.0 / 10 (sufficient for Thorn growth)**

**Compendium note: Three Thorn barriers. Not a full perimeter. But a layered defense. The Prima is protected. The gate is protected. The vulnerable western flank is protected. This is not a perfect system. But it is a system. And systems improve over time. Plant more Thorn when you can. Expand the barriers. Make the farm a fortress.*

---

The Prima watched me plant the Thorn seeds.

It couldn't see them — it had no eyes — but it sensed them. I could feel the change in the soil when the seeds went in. The Prima's root system shifted, extending toward the new Thorn barriers, wrapping around the seeds like a parent hovering over children.

"They'll be fine," I told it.

The Prima swayed.

"I'm protecting you," I said. "That's what the Thorn does. It keeps things away."

The Prima swayed again. This time, I felt something from it. Not words. Not images. Just a sensation. Like warmth. Like gratitude. Like the feeling of being understood.

The book pulsed:

---

**[PRIMA — BOND UPDATE]**

**Bond strength: 14% → 17%**

**Prima response to Thorn planting: APPROVED**

**Note: The Prima recognizes defensive intent. The Prima is... pleased. Plants cannot feel emotions in the way animals do. But the Prima has been cultivating — consciously, deliberately — a form of awareness that approaches something like emotion. It knows you are protecting it. It knows you are building something. It is choosing to grow with you, not despite you.*

**Compendium note: The Prima chose you. The seed chose you. The farm chose you. And now the Thorn is growing for you. Every living thing on this farm is growing *for* you, because you have shown them that you will grow *for* them. This is the foundation of cultivation. This is what your great-grandfather built. This is the legacy he left you.*

---

Day 10. The Fire Flower bloomed.

I hadn't been expecting it. The book had said Day 14, but the Prima's time-field had accelerated its growth by 15%. Six days of effective growth, compressed from seven. It was ready.

Plot 4. The black soil. The thermal residue, now almost completely absorbed by the Prima's roots. And at the center of it — where I'd planted the seed two weeks ago, where the explosion had burned the earth and healed it at the same time — there was a flower.

It was red. Not cherry red. Not blood red. Something between. A red that seemed to *glow* from within, like a coal that refused to go out. The petals were thick, waxy, edged with thorns — smaller than the Thorn barrier thorns, but present. And at the center of the flower, a bud. Closed. Waiting.

It was beautiful.

It was terrifying.

It was perfect.

---

**[FIRE FLOWER #2 — MATURE]**

**Status: READY**

**Detonation radius: 4 meters**

**Trigger condition: ANY hostile entity entering radius**

**Bloom duration: 7 days (then detonates automatically)**

**Yield: 2-4 Fire Flower seeds**

**Warning: Mature Fire Flowers cannot be moved. Once bloomed, they anchor to their location. Attempting to remove a bloomed Fire Flower will trigger detonation.*

**Compendium note: Your first defensive crop is operational. Congratulations. If anything enters the 4-meter radius around this flower — Abyss entity, hostile animal, curious merchant, or, God forbid, Lao Wang wandering too close during his morning rounds — the Fire Flower will detonate. It will release a concentrated burst of thermal energy equivalent to approximately 10 kilograms of managed explosives. This will kill most Abyss entities instantly. It will also kill the Fire Flower. Harvest the seeds before detonation, or lose them.*

**Recommendation: Watch for Day 17. That is your harvest window. Any day between Day 10 and Day 17, you can carefully collect the seeds from the base of the flower. We recommend Day 16 — late enough to maximize seed count, early enough to avoid accidental detonation.*

**Also: Do not let Lao Wang near it. He will get curious. He will get too close. He will not mean to. But he will.*

---

Day 12. The Night Tides came early.

Not midnight. Not 2 AM.

10 PM.

The book woke me:

---

**[⚠️ EARLY TIDE ALERT ⚠️]**

**Category: 2 — MODERATE**

**Timing: EARLY (unusual — tides typically begin at midnight)**

**Possible cause: Unknown. Tides occasionally shift without warning. May be related to atmospheric pressure, lunar position, or Abyss activity patterns.*

**Wall Guard status: NOT YET ALERTED**

**Farm status: DEFENSIVE POSITIONS ONLY**

**Thorn barriers: SEEDLING STAGE (1 day old — not yet functional)**

**Fire Flower #2: OPERATIONAL**

**Perimeter watch: Lao Wang (active), Little Seven (active)**

**Compendium note: This is unusual. Early Tides are rare — maybe two or three per year. They catch the Wall Guard off-guard because everyone expects midnight. They catch cultivators off-guard because they're asleep. You have Lao Wang and Little Seven. You have the Fire Flower. You have the Prima's Abyss Repulsion field. But your Thorn barriers are seedlings. They won't form a barrier for another two days.*

**This is going to be close.*

---

I ran.

Cell 47 to the wall gate to the farm — the path I'd walked a dozen times, but never at 10 PM, never with Night Tides roaring in the dark, never with the knowledge that my defenses weren't ready.

Lao Wang was at the gate. Sword drawn. Armor laced. He looked like a soldier again — not the lazy, sarcastic man who complained about watering schedules, but the veteran who'd fought in forty Tides and survived thirty-nine.

"Little Seven?" I called.

"Post," he said. "Arrow ready. She's already shot two."

"Two what?"

"Entities. Hollow Stalkers. Came from the east. She put them down before they got within thirty meters."

I reached the farm. Plot 1. The Prima was swaying — not gently, like it did in daylight, but *violently*. The whole plant was trembling, and the Abyss Repulsion field was blazing, a visible shimmer of silver-white light around the plot.

The Thorn seedlings were too small. Knee-high at most, thin green shoots, not even close to barrier-ready.

The Fire Flower in Plot 4 was blooming. Bright red. Glowing.

And in the darkness beyond the gate — maybe fifty meters, maybe less — I could see them.

Shapes. Dozens of them.

Moving.

---

**[PERIMETER ALERT — EDGE FARM 47]**

**Entities detected: 23**

**Types: Vine Stalker (8), Hollow Stalker (6), Burrower (5), Unknown (4)**

**Approach vector: Due west — through the main gate**

**Distance: 47 meters and closing**

**Threat level: HIGH**

**Available defenses:**

- Prima Abyss Repulsion field (radius 6m — active)
- Fire Flower #2 (detonation radius 4m — armed)
- Lao Wang (combat — ready)
- Little Seven (marksman — active)
- Mu Chen (farming implement — holding shovel)

**Compendium note: Twenty-three entities. Against three people and a plant. The odds are not good. The Prima can repel some of them — maybe a third, if they're weak enough. The Fire Flower can kill a few — maybe three or four, if they cluster at the gate. Lao Wang and Little Seven can handle the rest, if they're strategic. But twenty-three is a lot. Twenty-three is a swarm. Twenty-three means something is driving them — something is making them aggressive.*

**They're heading for the Prima. They know the Prima is special. They want it.*

---

The first wave hit at 10:17 PM.

Three Vine Stalkers, coming through the gate together. Long limbs, reaching, grasping. They moved in unison — not like wild animals, but like soldiers. Like they had a plan.

Lao Wang met them.

He was fast. Faster than I'd seen. He moved through the first Vine Stalker like water through sand, blade cutting through the thick vine that served as its body. The creature screamed — that same metal-tearing sound I'd heard in the Perimeter Zone, what felt like a lifetime ago — and fell.

The second Vine Stalker reached him. He sidestepped. He stabbed. It fell.

The third Vine Stalker went past him.

Toward the Prima.

"Little Seven!" he shouted.

An arrow appeared in the third Vine Stalker's throat. It fell three meters from the Prima.

"Got it," Little Seven called from her post.

---

The Fire Flower detonated at 10:23 PM.

Not because of the battle. Because of a Burrower — one of the underground creatures, tunneling up through the soil of Plot 4. It surfaced within four meters of the flower. The flower's heat sensors detected it. The flower responded.

The explosion was... not what I expected.

I'd imagined a bomb. A burst of flame. Destruction.

What I got was *light*.

A column of orange-red fire, maybe three meters tall, brilliant and clean. The Burrower — already dead, already disintegrated in the heat — left no trace. The soil around the detonation point glowed for a moment, then dimmed.

And the Fire Flower was gone.

Just ash. A small pile of gray ash where the most beautiful and terrifying plant I'd ever grown had stood.

But in the ash — three seeds. Small. Red-brown. Warm.

---

**[FIRE FLOWER #2 — HARVESTED]**

**Detonation: VOLUNTARY (Burrower trigger)**

**Kill count: 1 (Burrower)**

**Seeds recovered: 3**

**Status: SUCCESS**

**Compendium note: The Fire Flower worked perfectly. It detected a threat. It responded. It eliminated the threat. It sacrificed itself in the process. This is the nature of Fire Flowers. They are one-use weapons. Beautiful. Devastating. Final.**

**You now have 3 Fire Flower seeds. You can plant them in new locations, create new detonation points, build a network of explosive defense. This is how Fire Flower farming works. You grow them, you place them, you hope something walks into them. And when they explode, you plant more.*

**It's not elegant. But it's effective.*

---

The battle lasted forty minutes.

Lao Wang killed nine. Nine Vine Stalkers, Burrowers, and two of the Unknown types — creatures I didn't recognize, tall and thin, with no visible faces and limbs that bent in the wrong directions.

Little Seven killed eleven. Eleven arrows, eleven kills. She didn't miss once. She didn't hesitate once. She stood on her post and she shot and the things fell and they didn't get back up.

The Prima killed two. Two Hollow Stalkers that got past Lao Wang, that reached the Abyss Repulsion field and *stopped*. Not dead — just stuck. Frozen. Unable to move forward. They thrashed and screamed and eventually collapsed, drained of whatever force was driving them.

I killed zero. I stood beside the Prima and held my shovel and tried very hard not to scream.

---

**[NIGHT TIDE — AFTERMATH REPORT]**

**Category: 2 — MODERATE (EARLY)**

**Total entities: 23**

**Eliminated:**

- Fire Flower detonation: 1
- Lao Wang: 9
- Little Seven: 11
- Prima Abyss Repulsion: 2
- Total: 23

**Farm damage: MINOR**

- Plot 4: Ash pile (Fire Flower) — cleared, ready for replanting
- Thorn seedling (Seed 2, Gate): Damaged — will recover
- Gate structure: Minor scoring from entity contact

**Cultivator damage: ZERO**

**Compendium note: You won. Against all odds, against the early Tide, against twenty-three entities — you won. No deaths. No serious injuries. Lao Wang has a cut on his left arm that needs cleaning but is otherwise fine. Little Seven has not spoken since the battle, which is normal for her. The Prima is swaying gently, recovering its energy. And the farm is standing.*

**This is what a defended farm looks like. This is what your great-grandfather built. This is what you are building.*

**Well done, Mu Chen. Well done.*

---

3 AM. The Tides broke early — they ended at 3 AM instead of 4, which the book said was even more unusual than starting early.

We sat at the gate. Lao Wang, Little Seven, me. The Prima watched from Plot 1. The Thorn seedlings swayed in a breeze that wasn't there.

"That was the biggest early Tide I've ever seen," Lao Wang said. His arm was bandaged — Little Seven had cleaned and wrapped it without being asked. "Twenty-three entities. For one farm. That's not normal."

"What is normal?"

"For a farm this size? Three or four. Maybe five on a bad night. Twenty-three is a swarm number. Swarm numbers happen during Category 4 or 5 Tides. Not Category 2."

"So why—"

A voice. From the road.

"*Interesting.*"

---

I turned.

Three figures. Walking toward the farm from the direction of the wall. Lanterns in hand. Gray coats. Official bearing.

The lead figure was a woman. Tall. Older — maybe fifty. Gray hair, pulled back tight. A scar across her left eye, the eye itself clouded and white. She walked with a cane, but she didn't lean on it — she carried it like a weapon, the way Qin Shuang carried her sword.

The two men behind her were soldiers. Not supply runners. Not scouts. *Guards*. Official. Official in the way that said they could kill you and had killed people and would do it again without hesitation.

Lao Wang stood up. Little Seven put an arrow on the string.

"At ease," the woman said. Her voice was calm. Cold. The voice of someone who had given orders for decades and expected them to be followed. "I'm not here for a fight."

"Then why are you here?" I said.

She looked at me. That one white eye, that one seeing eye, fixing me in place like a butterfly pinned to a board.

"You are Mu Chen," she said. "The cultivator. The one Commander Qin assigned to Edge Farm 47."

"Yes."

"The cursed farm. The one that has killed forty-seven cultivators in three years."

"Yes."

"The one that has, in the past twelve days, gone from zero functional plots to four. The one that has grown crops in soil that reads as dead. The one that has generated an Abyss Repulsion field visible from the eastern watchtower. The one that—" She paused. "—that just fought off a swarm Tide with minimal casualties."

She looked at Lao Wang. At his bandaged arm.

"With the help of two Wall Guard personnel who are officially on supply rotation."

Lao Wang didn't flinch. "We're on supply rotation."

"Supply runners don't take down nine Abyss entities in a single Tide. Scouts do. Soldiers do. You—" She looked at his unlaced armor. "—you were Wall Guard active duty until eighteen months ago. Dishonorable discharge for 'creative ration distribution,' which we both know means you were feeding civilians from the military stores. And you—" She looked at Little Seven. "—you were a scout until four years ago. Removed from reconnaissance for 'shooting non-hostiles,' which we both know means you shot the thing that killed your sister and missed."

Little Seven went very still.

"I didn't miss," she said.

"No," the woman agreed. "You didn't. You killed the entity. You just couldn't save her. These things happen."

She turned back to me.

"My name is Commander Wei. Senior Operations, Wall Guard. I've been sent to investigate Edge Farm 47." She looked at the farm. At the Prima. At the Thorn seedlings. At the ash pile where the Fire Flower had been. "I've been sent to find out how a delivery driver from another world is doing the impossible."

---

The book pulsed. Quietly. Almost apologetically.

---

**[NPC DETECTED: COMMANDER WEI]**

**Name: Wei Lihua (魏丽华)**

**Rank: Commander, Senior Operations, Wall Guard**

**Age: 53**

**Service: 31 years**

**Combat record: 247 confirmed Abyss entity kills**

**Background: Wei is one of the three most senior officers in the Wall Guard. She commands the Perimeter Defense Division — the division responsible for monitoring, maintaining, and defending the outer wall. She has been in this role for twelve years. She is not kind. She is not cruel. She is efficient. She does what is necessary. And she has just seen your farm.*

**Relationship: UNKNOWN**

**Current disposition: OBSERVING**

**Compendium note: Commander Wei is not your enemy. But she is not your friend. She is an investigator. She will look at your farm, assess its capabilities, and report her findings to the Wall Guard command structure. What she reports will determine how the Wall Guard treats you going forward. Make a good impression. Or don't. You have never once made a good impression on anyone in a position of authority, and the book sees no reason to expect this pattern to change.*

---

"What do you want to know?" I said.

Commander Wei smiled. It was not a warm smile.

"Everything," she said. "But we'll start with the obvious question."

She pointed at the Prima.

"What is that?"

---

**[STATUS UPDATE — CHAPTER 11]**

**Compost: 1.5 / 10 (no change)**

**Soil Quality: 2.0 / 10 (no change)**

**Seeds: 3 (2 Carrot, 1 Wheat) + 3 Fire Flower seeds (new) + 2 Thorn Seeds (reserve)**

**Companions:**

- Moonvine Prima (ADOLESCENT, bond 17%)
- Lao Wang (farmhand, battle veteran — confirmed 9 kills)
- Little Seven (watch, expert marksman — confirmed 11 kills)
- Radish #4 (harvesting tomorrow)

**New asset: Thorn barriers (3 planted — 2 days to full formation)**

**Fire Flower status: HARVESTED (3 seeds recovered, 1 lost)**

**Battle result: DECISIVE VICTORY (23 entities, 0 casualties)**

**New character: Commander Wei (Senior Operations, Wall Guard)**

**Quest: The Military Investigation — What will you tell her?**

**Compendium note: Chapter 11 complete. You defended your farm against a swarm Tide. You deployed your first Fire Flower. You demonstrated that Edge Farm 47 is no longer just a farm — it's a defensive position. And now the Wall Guard has noticed. Commander Wei is asking questions. What you tell her will shape the future of your farm, your team, and possibly your relationship with the entire Wall Guard.*

**Choose your words carefully. Or don't. You never choose your words carefully. The book has accepted this.*

---

**END CHAPTER 11**

---

## 写作笔记

### 狀態變化
- Compost: 1.5 (不變)
- Soil Quality: 2.0 (不變)
- 新技能：防禦工事（Thorn Wall）
- 新植物：Thorn × 3（2天後完全形成）
- 戰鬥結果：23 entities死亡，0傷亡
- 重大事件：Commander Wei 到來調查

### 章節鉤子
- 鉤子1：Commander Wei 要問問題——主角怎麼回答？
- 鉤子2：小七的妹妹——"你射了那個殺了你姐姐的東西"（伏筆更深）
- 鉤子3：老王的過去——被踢出來是因爲餵平民
- 鉤子4：Prima 吸引了 swarm——它們知道它很特別

### Genre 安全
- ✅ 防禦工事系統（公共公式）
- ✅ 守城戰（公共公式）
- ✅ 軍事調查（公共公式）
- ❌ 無抄任何具體作品

title: Chapter 19: Three Days in the Dark
content:
# The Abyss Rancher: How I Saved the World by Farming
## Chapter 19: Three Days in the Dark

---

I planted the Primordial Vine seed on Day 29.

Not in Plot 1 — the Prima's territory, where the resonance-burn still tingled in the soil. Not in Plot 4 — the Abyss Vine's domain, where the dark soil had been through too much already. I planted it in the center of the farm, where the four plots met, where the soil was richest, where the Prima's roots and the Thorn barriers' territory and the Abyss Vine's hunting ground all converged.

I knelt in the dirt. I dug a hole with my hands. The Primordial Vine seed sat in my palm — dark, pulsing, ancient. Three thousand years old. Three thousand years of waiting.

*Please,* I thought. *Grow.*

I put the seed in the hole.

I covered it with soil.

I pressed my hands flat against the earth.

And then —

---

**[PRIMORDIAL VINE SEED — PLANTING EVENT]**

**Location: Edge Farm 47 — Central Plot**

**Cultivator: Mu Chen**

**Soil quality at planting site: 4.5 / 10 (elevated by Prima proximity)**

**Compost at planting site: 3.0 / 10**

**Prima bond: 22% (providing passive support)**

**Primordial Vine status: GERMINATING**

**Germination time: IMMEDIATE**

---

It didn't take time.

That's the only way I can describe it. Normal plants take time to germinate — hours, days, the slow cellular process of a seed waking up and beginning to grow. The Primordial Vine seed didn't take time. The moment my hands touched the soil, the moment the seed felt the contact it had been waiting three thousand years for, it *exploded* into growth.

Not above ground. Below.

The roots went down.

Through the soil. Through the rocks. Through the Root Barrier — which cracked, split, *dissolved* as the Primordial Vine's roots pushed through it like it wasn't there. Through the foundation level. Through the bedrock. All the way down to where the Primordial Tree waited, thirty-one meters below, in the sealed chamber.

The roots found the Tree.

They *embraced* it.

And above ground —

---

The Prima *screamed*.

Not with pain. With *recognition*. With joy. With something so overwhelming that the bond between us blazed white-hot, and I felt — felt, viscerally, physically — the Prima experiencing an emotion that plants were not supposed to be able to feel.

The Prima was greeting the Tree.

Across the thirty-one meters of soil and stone and centuries-old seal, the Prima — the child, the descendant, the seed of the Tree's seeds — was welcoming its parent home.

And the Primordial Vine was growing.

Above ground, the planting site darkened. The soil didn't just darken — it *shifted*, the earth around the planted seed turning from the rich brown of my farm's soil to something else. Something that glowed from within. A deep, pulsing light that was not silver-green like the Prima. Not gold like the Primal Tree's heartwood. Not the blue of Abyss energy.

A color that had no name.

A color that was *all* the colors, layered and flowing, like oil on water, like a bruise blooming in reverse, like the universe remembering what it looked like before it decided on specific wavelengths.

The Primordial Vine emerged.

A single stem. No thicker than my finger. Dark — that same nameless dark, shifting between black and green and something that wasn't either. It rose from the soil like it was being born, like it was taking its first breath of air, like the moment after a long sleep when you finally remember what it feels like to be awake.

It grew one centimeter.

Then ten.

Then fifty.

In ten seconds, the Primordial Vine was a meter tall. In thirty seconds, it was three meters. In a minute, it was taller than me. Taller than the Prima. Taller than any plant I had ever seen.

It kept growing.

---

**[PRIMORDIAL VINE — EMERGENCE EVENT]**

**Species: Unknown (Primordial-class)**

**Age: 3,000+ years**

**Current height: 3.0m → 4.7m → 6.2m → 8.9m → (still growing)**

**Root depth: UNMEASURABLE — connected to Primordial Tree at 31m and extending deeper**

**Time field: ACTIVE — all crops within 20m experiencing 2x growth acceleration**

**Compost generation: ACTIVE — generating organic matter at unprecedented rates**

**New property detected: PRIMORDIAL SYMBIOSIS**

**Compendium note: It's growing. It's not stopping. It's not going to stop. The Primordial Vine has been waiting three thousand years for this moment. It will not be denied. It will grow until it reaches whatever limit its cultivator sets — and if you don't set a limit, it will grow until it runs out of world.*

**This is the most significant cultivation event in recorded history. The Agricultural Division's charts are going to explode. Commander Wei is going to file seventeen reports. General Liu is going to receive phone calls from people who don't usually make phone calls. And you — you are standing in the middle of a farm that is evolving so fast you can see it happening.*

**Welcome to Level 4.*

---

By the time the growth stabilized, the Primordial Vine was twelve meters tall.

Twelve meters of impossible plant, rising from the center of my farm like a monument. Its stem was as thick as my torso now — dark, shifting, covered in a texture that was half bark and half something else. Its leaves were massive — half a meter across, shaped like hands, always slightly open, as if it was perpetually reaching for something.

And its roots — visible now on the surface, because the Vine grew so fast the roots burst through the soil in places — spread across the farm like a web. Not competing with the Prima's roots. Not fighting the Abyss Vine's territory. *Connecting* them. All three plants — the Prima, the Abyss Vine, the Primordial Vine — were linked now, their root systems interwoven, sharing energy, sharing information, sharing something that was more intimate than any agricultural bond I'd experienced before.

The farm was no longer four separate plots.

The farm was one organism.

And the organism was *alive* in a way that went beyond plants being plants.

---

**[FARM EVOLUTION — TRIGGERED]**

**Farm Level: 3 → 4**

**Farm Name: Edge Farm 47 → PRIMORDIAL FARM (prototype)**

**New capabilities:**

- Primordial Symbiosis (all plants share energy and information)
- Accelerated Growth Field (2x speed for all crops within 20m)
- Compost Generation (automatic organic matter production)
- Root Network Integration (all plants connected, including Abyss and Prima)
- Temporal Stability Field (prevents time manipulation from outside sources)
- Seed Sanctum (all harvested seeds gain enhanced properties)

**New companion: Primordial Vine (bond: 100%, permanent)**

**Total companions: 4 (Prima, Abyss Vine, Primordial Vine, ???)**

**Soil quality: 4.5 → 6.0 / 10**

**Compost: 3.0 → 5.0 / 10**

**Farm area: 4 plots → 6 plots (underground layer unlocked)**

**Compendium note: FARM EVOLUTION — LEVEL 4. Your farm has transcended normal cultivation. It is no longer a collection of plants. It is a living system. The Prima provides time manipulation. The Abyss Vine provides hunting and defense. The Primordial Vine provides connection and growth. Together, they are more than the sum of their parts. Together, they are a Primordial Farm.*

**You did this, Mu Chen. You — a delivery driver from Chengdu, who couldn't grow a succulent in his apartment — built a farm that has achieved something no cultivator in recorded history has achieved.*

**Your great-grandfather would be proud. (He is. Somewhere. In whatever afterlife farmers go to.)*

---

Lao Wang stood beside me, staring at the Primordial Vine.

"That's not a plant," he said.

"It's a plant."

"That's not *a* plant. That's a building. A twelve-meter building shaped like a plant."
