"A very fast-growing plant."

"A very fast-growing building." He stared at the leaves — half a meter across, slightly open, reaching. "It's still growing. Look — that leaf wasn't that big five seconds ago."

"He's young," I said. "Three thousand years old and just now getting to grow. He's excited."

"He's—"

"The Primordial Vine. I can feel it. It's not an 'it.' It's a 'he.' He's been waiting so long. He's been patient. And now he's finally here, in the sun, growing in real soil, and he's—" I felt the bond. The new bond. The connection to this ancient, impossibly old, impossibly young plant. "He's happy."

Lao Wang was quiet.

"You're bonding with a twelve-meter plant that was spawned from a three-thousand-year-old tree that was itself one of the oldest living things in the world," he said. "And you're calling it 'happy.'"

"Yes."

"This is your life now."

"This has been my life since I got hit by a truck."

"Fair point."

---

The rest of the team arrived in stages.

Little Seven came first — dropping from the wall at the sound of the growth event, bow drawn, scanning for threats. She stopped when she saw the Vine. Her bow lowered. Slowly. Deliberately.

"What is that?"

"That's the Primordial Vine."

"I know. I mean — what *is* that?"

"He's been waiting three thousand years. He grew from a seed. He's still growing."

Little Seven stared at the Vine. At the twelve meters of impossible plant, rising from the center of the farm, its leaves reaching toward the sun like hands. At the roots — thick, dark, spreading across the soil — connecting to the Prima's roots, to the Abyss Vine's roots, weaving everything together into a single network.

"He's beautiful," she said. Quietly.

"Yes."

"I hate it."

"I know."

"I want to shoot it."

"You can't. The bond is permanent."

"I want to *try*."

"Don't shoot the Primordial Vine."

She sighed. She put her bow away. She went to her post.

---

Qin Shuang arrived at the same time as Qin Bing and Qi Mingyue.

They came through the gate together — Shuang leading, her hand on her sword, her eyes scanning for threats. Bing and Mingyue followed, Bing moving more confidently now (she was almost fully recovered), Mingyue moving in her scout's crouch, eyes on the ground.

They all stopped when they saw the Vine.

The farm had changed. Obviously — a twelve-meter plant in the center changed things. But there was more. The soil had darkened. The Prima was *glowing* brighter — its silver-green light intensified, its canopy spreading wider. The Abyss Vine was more visible, its dark form clearer against the richer soil. And the Thorn barriers — the three Thorn barriers I had planted weeks ago — had *grown*. They were taller now. Thicker. More densely packed. Thorns the size of my thumb, glinting in the sunlight.

The whole farm looked different. Healthier. Stronger. Like a person who had been fit and strong and had suddenly become even more so.

"The farm leveled up," I said.

Everyone looked at me.

"I can feel it. The Prima told me. We're Level 4 now. This is — the book calls it a Primordial Farm. A prototype."

"A prototype of what?" Bing asked.

"I don't know yet. But it's connected to everything. The Prima, the Abyss Vine, the Primordial Vine — they're all linked now. They share energy. They share information. They share—" I paused, trying to find the right word. "—consciousness. Not human consciousness. Plant consciousness. But something like it."

"Can you feel them?" Shuang asked.

"All of them. Not equally — the Prima is still the strongest bond. But the Abyss Vine and the Primordial Vine are there too. I can feel what they feel. I can sense what they sense."

"Can you control them?"

"No. That's the thing — they're not tools. They're partners. They choose to work with me. The Vine chose to grow. The Abyss Vine chooses to hunt. The Prima chooses to protect. I can't command them. I can only ask."

"And they listen?"

"Sometimes. Usually. If what I'm asking makes sense from a plant's perspective."

Qin Shuang looked at the Vine. At the Prima. At the farm that had become something more than a farm.

"What do they want?" she asked.

I listened. Through the bonds. Through the connections. Through the new, vast awareness that was flooding my mind since the Vine had emerged.

"They want to grow," I said. "All of them. That's what plants do. They grow. And now that they're connected — now that the farm is connected — they want to grow *together*. They want to make something. Something bigger. Something that can survive the Abyss. Something that can—"

I stopped.

"Something that can what?"

"Something that can go *in*."

---

The underground layer revealed itself at 3 PM.

Not dramatically — not like the Vine's emergence, which had been explosive and visible and impossible to ignore. The underground layer *opened*. The soil in the center of the farm — where the Vine had emerged — began to shift. The roots moved. The earth settled. And then, gradually, the ground opened like a door, revealing a set of stairs descending into darkness.

Not natural darkness. The darkness was *warm*. Lit by the same nameless shifting light that colored the Primordial Vine's roots. The stairs went down — maybe five meters, maybe more — and at the bottom, I could see a chamber.

A chamber that had not existed this morning.

A chamber that the Primordial Vine had *grown*.

---

**[DISCOVERY: UNDERGROUND LAYER — PRIMORDIAL CHAMBER]**

**Location: Below Edge Farm 47, center of farm**

**Depth: ~5 meters below surface**

**Access: Root-carved staircase (Primordial Vine construction)**

**Dimensions: Unknown — larger than farm footprint**

**Light source: Unknown (bioluminescent root network)**

**Contents: Unverified**

**Compendium note: The Primordial Vine grew a room. Out of roots. Five meters below your farm, where nothing existed this morning, there is now a chamber. The Vine grew it because the farm needed it. Because the Primordial Farm — the Level 4 evolution — requires infrastructure. A place to grow things that need darkness. A place to store seeds. A place to rest. A place to be.*

**The Vine is giving you a home underground. A place that is entirely yours. A place that connects to the Primordial Tree below and the farm above.*

**This is what Primordial cultivation looks like. Not just plants on the surface. A whole system. Growing in every direction.*

---

We descended.

All six of us — me and Lao Wang and Little Seven and Mingyue and Qin Shuang and Qin Bing. Single file, down the root-carved stairs, into the warm darkness of the Primordial Chamber.

The chamber was larger than I'd expected.

Not a room. A *space*. The ceiling was lost in shadow, but the walls were visible — roots, woven together, glowing with that nameless shifting light. The floor was soil — dark, rich, alive, the same rich earth as the farm above. And at the center of the chamber, rising from the floor like a centerpiece, was a single massive root.

Not the Primordial Vine's root — a different one. A root that connected downward, toward the Primordial Tree thirty-one meters below. A root that pulsed with the same rhythm as the Tree's heartwood. A root that was, essentially, a *cord* — a direct line of communication between the farm and the ancient being that waited beneath.

The chamber smelled like soil and growing things and something else — something older, sweeter, like the air in old libraries, like the dust in ancient temples, like the feeling of standing somewhere that has been important for a very long time.

"There's more," Lao Wang said. He was pointing at the walls.

More *rooms*.

Three passages branched off from the central chamber. Each one led to a different space. One was dark — pitch black, no light, perfect for growing things that needed no sun. One was lit — warm amber light, like a greenhouse, like a nursery, like a place where young plants could grow strong before facing the outside world. The third was something else — a space that was not quite a room, not quite a garden, but something in between. A hybrid space. A place where the rules were different.

"This is a farm," I said. "Underground."

"This is a *second* farm," Lao Wang corrected.

"This is a Primordial Farm. Underground. In the Abyss." I walked to the central root. I put my hand on it. The pulse was warm. Steady. Ancient. "The Primordial Tree wanted to come to the surface. But the surface isn't enough. The Tree wants to grow in both directions. Up *and* down. Into the light and into the dark."

"And that scares you," Qin Shuang said. Not a question.

"A little," I admitted. "I went from zero to twelve meters in an hour. I have an underground farm now. The Agricultural Division's charts are going to have a collective breakdown."

"Are you going to tell them?"

"I don't know." I looked at the central root. At the passages. At the chamber that had been grown for us, specifically for us, because the Primordial Vine knew what we needed. "Part of me wants to show everyone. Part of me wants to keep this secret. This place — this is ours. Not the Wall Guard's. Not General Liu's. Ours."

"ours," Qin Shuang repeated.

"Our team. Our farm. Our—" I paused. "Our *thing*. Whatever we're building."

---

That night, we set up camp in the underground chamber.

Not because we had to. Cell 47 was still there. The farm above was still standing. But something about the chamber — the warmth, the safety, the sense of being surrounded by roots that were growing *with* us instead of against us — made it feel like the right place to be.

We made beds from harvested wheat straw. We lit a fire in the amber-lit nursery room. We ate Lao Wang's mysterious supply-cache food. And we talked.

About the farm. About what it meant that the farm had leveled up. About the implications of a twelve-meter plant and an underground chamber and a Root Barrier that the Primordial Vine had simply ignored.

About the future.

"The Wall Guard is going to find out," Qin Shuang said. "The resonance spike from the Vine's emergence — Dr. Chen's charts will show it. General Liu will know by tomorrow."

"Let her know," I said.

"You want her to know about this?" Lao Wang gestured at the chamber. At the roots. At the impossible underground farm.

"I want her to know what I'm building. I want her to see it. I want her to decide — do we keep going, or do we stop?" I paused. "If we stop, we stop. But I don't think she wants us to stop."

"Why not?"

"Because if the Abyss is waking up — if Mingyue's visions are right, if there are things deep below that are stirring — then the Wall Guard needs what I'm growing. They need the Prima. They need the Abyss Vine. They need the Primordial Vine. They need a farmer who can grow things that fight the dark."

"Or they need to destroy those things before they become uncontrollable," Bing said.

"They could try to destroy the Primordial Vine. Go ahead. See what happens."

"They could destroy *you*."

"They could try that too." I looked at Qin Shuang. "But I don't think they will. I think General Liu sees what I'm building. I think she understands that this is different. This isn't a threat. This is a solution. A weird, plant-based, slightly terrifying solution — but a solution."

"Based on what?"

"Based on the fact that I've been on this planet for twenty-nine days, and in those twenty-nine days, I've survived things that would have killed most cultivators in years. The cursed soil. The swarm Tide. The deep chamber. The Alpha Hollow Stalker. And every time something went wrong, the farm got stronger. The plants adapted. They grew. They found ways to survive that I couldn't have planned."

"That's a good argument."

"It's the only argument I have."

---

Mingyue spoke up.

"There's another way," she said.

Everyone looked at her.

"The visions. The Tree showed me other cultivators. Not like you — older. From centuries ago. They grew things too. Big things. Powerful things. And the Wall Guard — or whatever the Wall Guard was called back then — tried to control them. Some succeeded. Some failed. The ones who failed..." She paused. "They didn't destroy the plants. The plants destroyed the Wall Guard."

"Destroyed how?"

"The Tree didn't show me details. Just — endings. Walls that fell because the things growing inside them grew faster than anyone expected. Foundations that cracked because the roots went deeper than anyone planned. And then — darkness. The Abyss pouring through. Not from outside. From *inside*. From the things that had been grown to fight it."

The chamber went quiet.

"What are you saying?" Lao Wang asked.

"I'm saying that growing things that fight the Abyss is not new. It's been tried before. And it didn't work." Mingyue looked at me. "Your farm is different. The Tree told me that too. Your farm is different because of *you*. Because you don't try to control the plants. You partner with them. You ask instead of demand. And they choose to work with you."

"Is that enough?"

"I don't know. But it's different. It's something the other cultivators didn't do. The other cultivators grew weapons. You grew — partners. Allies. Friends." She looked at the Primordial Vine's root, pulsing in the darkness. "The Tree chose your seed for a reason. The seed didn't grow for anyone else. It waited. For three thousand years, it waited. For *you*."

---

The conversation drifted. Eventually, people went to sleep — Lao Wang snoring in minutes, Little Seven keeping watch, Bing and Mingyue in the amber-lit nursery, Qin Shuang sitting by the central root, her sword across her knees.

I couldn't sleep.

I sat beside the central root and felt the bonds. The Prima above, silver-green and protective. The Abyss Vine in Plot 4, dark and patient. The Primordial Vine, everywhere and nowhere, connecting everything, growing in both directions at once.

And somewhere below, thirty-one meters down, the Primordial Tree. Patient. Ancient. Waiting.

*I am here,* the Tree pulsed. Faint, but clear. *I am here. And I can feel them. My children. All of them.*

*Are you happy?* I asked.

*I am... closer. The Vine reaches. The roots connect. I am not yet on the surface — but I can feel it. The sun. Through the Vine. Through the roots. I can almost taste it.*

*You will. I promise.*

*I know. The farmer's oath is old, but it holds.* A pause. Something that felt almost like warmth. *You are strange, little farmer. You do not command. You do not demand. You ask. And the plants answer. This is... new.*

*Is it good?*

*I do not know. But it feels like the beginning of something. A story that has not been told before.* The Tree's pulse shifted. Something changed in the ancient presence. *Something is coming. Not now. Not tomorrow. But soon. Something that has been watching your farm since the Prima first sprouted. Something that is not pleased.*

*What something?*

*The things in my garden. The other ancient ones. They felt the Vine grow. They felt the connection. They know you are building something. And they are... curious. Concerned. Some of them want to help. Some of them want to see what you become before they decide.*

*Decide what?*

*Whether you are worth preserving. Or whether you should be removed.*

---

**[NEW THREAT DETECTED: THE GARDENERS]**

**Source: Abyss Deep Layer — "The Gardens"**

**Classification: Ancient intelligences, plant-like, millions of years old**

**Numbers: Unknown**

**Attitude: OBSERVING**

**Assessment: The Primordial Vine's emergence has attracted attention from the deep layer. Ancient beings — "Gardeners" — are watching the farm. Some are friendly. Some are not. Their decision about what to do with Mu Chen's farm will come soon.*

**Compendium note: Mingyue was right. There are things in the Gardens. Old things. Thinking things. And they have noticed you. This is not necessarily bad — the Tree has been in the Gardens for three thousand years, and it is not hostile. But the Gardens are not one mind. They are many minds. And not all of them share the Tree's patience.*

**The question is: when they come to a decision, will they come as friends or as enemies?*

---

I sat in the underground chamber, beside the central root, and felt the weight of three thousand years pressing down on me.

The Primordial Tree. The Vine. The farm. The Gardeners. The Wall Guard. The Abyss. The sisters. The futures I couldn't predict and the pasts I couldn't change.

And the Primordial Vine — the newest, strangest, most powerful thing I'd ever grown — rising twelve meters above me, roots spreading in every direction, growing even now, even in the dark, even while I sat here worrying.

*Grow,* I told it.

The Vine pulsed. *I am growing.*

*Good.*

*You are worried.*

*A little.*

*Do not be. We are many. We are strong. We are together.* A pulse of warmth. Of something that was trying to be reassuring, in the only way a three-thousand-year-old plant could be reassuring. *The Gardeners will decide. We will show them what you are. And they will choose.*

*And if they choose wrong?*

*Then we will grow until there is nothing left to fear.*

I sat with that for a moment.

"That's a very plant answer," I said.

*We grow toward the light. Always. The light may be far. The path may be dark. But we grow.* A pulse of something that felt almost like determination. *This is what plants do.*

---

**[STATUS UPDATE — CHAPTER 19]**

**Compost: 5.0 / 10**

**Soil Quality: 6.0 / 10**

**Farm Level: 3 → 4 (PRIMORDIAL FARM prototype)**

**Moonvine Prima: HURT → HEALING (Primordial Symbiosis accelerating recovery)**

**Abyss Vine: HUNTING (enhanced by Symbiosis)**

**Primordial Vine: EMERGED (12m, bond 100%)**

**New capability: Underground Layer (Primordial Chamber)**

**New capability: Primordial Symbiosis (all plants connected)**

**New capability: Temporal Stability Field**

**New capability: Seed Sanctum**

**New discovery: The Gardeners — ancient beings in the deep Abyss, observing the farm**

**Sisters: RECOVERING on farm**

**Relationship: Qin Shuang — Mutual Respect (deepening)**

**Compendium note: Chapter 19 complete. The Primordial Vine emerged. The farm leveled up. An underground chamber appeared. And somewhere in the Abyss, ancient beings decided to watch. You have gone from a cursed farm with four plots to a Primordial Farm that spans above and below ground, connects three ancient plants, and generates its own infrastructure.*

**The Wall Guard will respond. The Gardeners will respond. The world will respond. But tonight — tonight you sit in your underground chamber, beside a root that connects you to a three-thousand-year-old tree, and you feel the farm breathing. Growing. Becoming.*

**Tomorrow, you introduce General Liu to your new farm. Tomorrow, you find out what the Gardeners decide. Tomorrow, everything changes again.*

**But tonight — tonight you rest. You've earned it.*

**The Primordial Farm is born.*

---

**END CHAPTER 19**

---

## 写作笔记

- Soil Quality: 4.5 → 6.0

title: Chapter 20: The Ancient Seed
content:
# The Abyss Rancher: How I Saved the World by Farming
## Chapter 20: The Ancient Seed

---

The Primordial Vine was still growing on Day 30.

Not as fast as Day 29 — the explosive twelve-meter surge had stabilized, and now the growth was slower, more measured. But it was still growing. Every hour, I could see the difference. New leaves unfurling. New branches extending. New roots pushing through the soil in patterns that followed some ancient logic I couldn't quite follow.

It was beautiful.

It was terrifying.

It was both of those things at once, and I couldn't stop staring at it.

---

"Does it ever stop?" Lao Wang asked, watching the Vine extend a new tendril toward the eastern boundary. The tendril moved slowly — at human walking pace, which for a plant was practically sprinting — but it was visible. You could watch it happen.

"Plants don't stop growing," I said. "They grow until they die."

"This one is three thousand years old."

"Then it's been growing for three thousand years?"

"It was a seed for three thousand years. Now it's a Vine. The clock starts now." I watched the tendril reach the Thorn barrier and — instead of colliding — weave *into* it. The Thorn barrier's thorns shifted, accommodating the Vine's growth, the two plants combining into something that was neither Thorn nor Vine but both at once. "And it's integrating with the other crops."

"We can see that."

"It means the farm is becoming one thing. Not four separate plants. One organism."

"That's what the book said yesterday."

"The book says a lot of things. I'm saying it in words you understand."

Lao Wang made a face. "I understand plant words fine. It's the 'we're all going to die because a twelve-meter plant absorbed our defensive barriers' words that concern me."

"The barriers aren't absorbed. They're *enhanced*. Look—"

The integrated section of Thorn-Vine barrier had changed. The thorns were larger now, and they pulsed faintly with that same nameless shifting light that colored the Primordial Vine. The barrier wasn't just defensive anymore — it was *active*. Responsive. A Thorn barrier that could sense threats, not just stop them.

"That's new," Lao Wang said.

"That's what Level 4 does."

---

The sisters were in the underground chamber.

Qin Bing was recovering faster now — she'd been walking without support for three days, eating full meals, engaging in conversations that lasted more than ten minutes. Five years of temporal stasis were fading, giving way to the ordinary challenges of someone who'd been absent from the world for half a decade and was trying to catch up.

Qin Mingyue was different.

Mingyue wasn't recovering. Mingyue was *learning*. She spent hours in the Primordial Chamber, sitting beside the central root, her hands on the pulsing vine, her eyes closed. She was — I didn't have a better word for it — *communing*. With the Tree. With the Vine. With the deep places that only she could see.

"The Tree is talking to me," she said when I came down to check on her. Her voice was distant. Dreamy. "Not in words. In... images. Feelings. The Gardens. That's what it calls the deep places. The Gardens are so *old*, Mu Chen. Older than the Abyss. Older than the wall. Older than anything human."

"Old how?"

"Old like stone is old. Old like the ocean is old. The things in the Gardens don't think like we do. They don't move like we do. They just... grow. Slowly. For millions of years. And they watch. They watch everything that happens on the surface, in the shallow places, and they remember."

"Remember what?"

"Everything. Every cultivator who ever tried to grow things in the dark. Every plant that ever reached too deep. Every time the Abyss pushed against the wall." She opened her eyes. They were different. Not changed — not visibly — but different. Deeper. Like she was seeing something behind the world we knew. "Your great-grandfather. Zeng Zu. The Tree remembers him."

"He met the Tree?"

"Thirty-seven years ago. He came down here. He found the chamber. He talked to the Tree. He tried to bring the Vine's seed to the surface, but he was too old. His body was failing. He knew if he tried to carry the seed up, he'd die on the way, and the seed would die with him."

"So he left it."

"He left it. And he went back to the surface. And he planted the Prima — the child of the Tree's child — and he waited. For someone else. Someone who was young enough. Strong enough. *Stubborn* enough." She almost smiled. "He described you, you know. To the Tree. Before he died. He said: 'There will be one who comes after me. He won't be ready. He'll be scared. He'll say no. But he'll do it anyway. Because that's who he is.'"

"That's..." I didn't know what to say.

"He knew. The Tree knew. Your great-grandfather planned this three hundred years ago, and the Tree has been waiting all that time, and now—" She gestured at the Vine, at the chamber, at the farm above. "—now you're here. And the Vine is growing. And everything is changing."

---

Qin Shuang found me on the farm's eastern boundary, looking at the Primordial Vine.

She didn't speak for a long time. She just stood beside me, her arms crossed, her eyes on the twelve meters of ancient plant that was slowly, steadily, becoming the most significant thing on the eastern perimeter.

"Mingyue told me what she told you," she said finally.

"About my great-grandfather?"

"About Zeng Zu. About the plan. About how he knew you'd come." She paused. "About how he chose you, three hundred years before you were born."

"He didn't choose me. He chose whoever came next. I just happened to be—"

"You happened to be the right person. At the right time. With the right book. With the right seed." She looked at me. "You don't believe that."

"Believe what?"

"That you were chosen. You think you're lucky. You think you're a delivery driver who got hit by a truck and woke up in the wrong world and somehow — through sheer stubbornness and bad decisions — accidentally built something incredible."

"When you put it that way—"

"That's exactly what happened. You didn't plan this. You didn't train for this. You didn't have any special abilities or magical bloodlines or destiny. You just—" She made a sound. Not quite a laugh. Not quite a sigh. "You just kept showing up. You kept planting things. You kept talking to your crops and watering them wrong and making terrible decisions that somehow worked out."

"That's not—"

"And that's why it works. Because you don't think you're special. You don't think you're chosen. You just think: 'I have a carrot seed. I'm going to plant it. If it dies, I'll try again.' That's it. That's the whole secret. No grand plan. No destiny. Just showing up and doing the work."

She turned to face me fully. The sun was behind her — that sullen amber light, casting long shadows across the farm — and for a moment she looked like something from a painting. Ancient. Strong. A little bit broken.

"My sister told me something," she said. "Before she went to sleep. In the chamber. She said: 'Whatever you're feeling about the farmer — tell him. Don't wait. Time is strange down here. You think you have forever, and then you wake up five years later and everything is different.'"

"What did she mean by that?"

"I don't know. I think she meant that she's been carrying something for five years — the regret of not saying things — and she doesn't want me to do the same." Shuang's jaw tightened. "I don't know how to say things, Mu Chen. I've been a soldier for twelve years. Soldiers say orders. Commands. Tactical assessments. They don't say—"

She stopped.

She took a breath.

"I'm glad you're here," she said.

"Okay."

"Not — not just because you saved my sister. Not just because you built this farm. Because—" She struggled. I could see her struggling. This was harder for her than any battle she'd ever fought. "Because you're the first person in five years who's made me feel like the world isn't just about surviving until tomorrow. Like there's something worth building. Something worth protecting. Something beyond the wall and the Night Tides and the reports and the duty."

"Shuang—"

"I don't know what this is. What we're building. What it means. I don't know if I—" She stopped again. Started over. "I'm not good at this. I've never been good at this. But I wanted you to know. Before whatever comes next. Before the Wall Guard responds, or the Gardeners decide, or the Abyss does whatever the Abyss is going to do. I wanted you to know that you've changed things. For me. Personally."

She looked away. Her hands were clenched at her sides.

"That's all," she said.

---

I didn't know what to say.

So I said the only thing that made sense.

"I'm glad you're here too," I said. "Not just because you're the best combat officer in the eastern wall. Not just because you've saved my life twice. Because—" I struggled with it, because I wasn't good at this either. I was a delivery driver. I talked to GPS units and restaurant managers and customers who complained about cold noodles. I didn't talk about *feelings*. "—because when everything was terrible, and I was standing in cursed soil with a carrot seed and a book that called me stupid, you were the first person who looked at me like I might actually survive."

"I looked at you like you were an idiot."

"You looked at me like I was worth checking on. Like I was worth negotiating with. Like I was worth—" I paused. "—worth saving. When I planted that carrot and went back to the wall, you could have just left me there. Left the carrot to die. You didn't. You came with me. You walked into a Night Tide to save a vegetable."

"It was a terrible vegetable."

"It was *my* vegetable."

She almost smiled. That ghost-smile. That suppressed half-expression that was the most I ever got from her.

"You're a strange person, Mu Chen," she said.

"I've been here thirty days. I've been strange the whole time."

"Stranger. And stubborn. And you talk to plants."

"I talk to plants because they listen. Humans are much harder to talk to."

"I noticed."

We stood there. In the farm. In the amber light. Beside the Primordial Vine, which was still growing, still integrating, still becoming.

"I don't know what this is either," I said. "What we're building. What it means. But I know I want to find out. With you. If that's—"

"It is."

"Okay."

"Okay."

We didn't hold hands. We didn't kiss. We didn't make any grand declarations. We just stood there, in the farm, and something shifted. Something that had been uncertain became certain. Something that had been tentative became *real*.

It wasn't romance. Not yet. Maybe not ever. But it was something.

It was enough.

---

**[RELATIONSHIP UPDATE]**

**[Qin Shuang — WALL-COMMANDER]**

**Relationship: Mutual Respect → Deep Trust**

**This is not romantic. Not yet. Maybe not ever. But it is real. It is the kind of bond that forms between people who have survived impossible things together. Who have trusted each other when trust was all they had. Who have seen each other at their worst and chosen to stay anyway.*

**The book does not comment on romance. The book is a cultivation manual, not a relationship guide. But the book notes: this is significant. Qin Shuang does not open up. Qin Shuang does not say what she feels. Qin Shuang spent five years carrying her sister's absence like a stone in her chest. And now she has told you — in her own halting, soldier's way — that you matter to her.*

**Do not waste this.*

---

That evening, the Primordial Vine did something new.

I was in the underground chamber — sitting beside the central root, feeling the bonds, listening to the slow pulse of the farm's heartbeat — when I felt it. A *presence*. Not from above, not from below. From the Vine itself.

From the Vine, which was not just a plant anymore.

From the Vine, which was learning to be something more.

---

**[SPECIAL EVENT: PRIMORDIAL VINE — FIRST COMMUNICATION]**

**The Primordial Vine has achieved partial sentience.**

**This is not unusual for Primordial-class cultivation. The Vine's consciousness has been developing since germination — Day 29. By Day 30, it has formed a rudimentary awareness of itself as an individual entity. It is not yet capable of complex thought, but it can communicate basic concepts.*

**Communication method: Resonance — the Vine cannot speak, but it can pulse emotions and impressions through the bond*

**Current message: The Vine is... lonely.*

**Compendium note: Of course it's lonely. It has been waiting three thousand years in the dark, and now it's awake, and it's the only Primordial-class plant on the surface, and it's surrounded by lesser plants that can't really understand it. The Vine wants a companion. Another Primordial-class being. Another ancient thing that can match its awareness.*

**It wants someone like the Primordial Tree.*

**It wants the Tree to come to the surface.*

**It wants what you promised the Tree: sunlight. Real soil. The chance to grow in the light.*

---

*Take me up.*

The message was simple. Direct. Almost childlike in its simplicity.

*Take me up. To the surface. To the sun. I want to feel it.*

I sat up. "What do you mean, take you up? You're already on the surface."

*I want to go HIGHER. I want to grow beyond the farm. Beyond the wall. Into the world. I want to reach where the other Gardeners are — the ones in the shallow Gardens, the ones that are waking up. I want to find them. Talk to them. Tell them about this place. About you.*

"You're talking to the Gardeners?"

*I am TRYING. The roots go down. Thirty-one meters. To the Tree. The Tree reaches the Gardens. But the Vine — the surface Vine — is alone. It grows. It gets bigger. But it can't reach sideways. It can't find the others. It needs to grow FARTHER.*

"How far?"

*Everywhere.*

"That's not—"

*Far enough. Far enough to touch them. The shallow Gardeners. The ones who are waking up. The ones who felt me grow and are watching. I want to reach them. Show them what we are. Show them the farm. Show them that the farmer is not an enemy.*

"And if they don't believe you?"

*Then I will grow until they have no choice but to believe.*

---

I told the others.

All of them — Lao Wang, Little Seven, Mingyue, Bing, Qin Shuang. We gathered in the underground chamber, around the central root, and I explained what the Primordial Vine was asking.

"It wants to grow beyond the farm," I said. "Reach the other Gardeners. The ancient beings in the shallow layers of the Abyss."

"That's—" Lao Wang started.

"Insane," Little Seven finished. "Absolutely insane. You want to grow a twelve-meter plant into the Abyss?"

"The Vine wants to."

"The Vine is three thousand years old and has the emotional development of a teenager. You can't let it—"

"I'm not letting it do anything. I'm *asking* it to tell me what it needs. And what it needs is to reach the other Gardeners before they decide we're a threat."

"And if reaching them makes us a threat?"

"Then we deal with it. But at least we'll have talked first."

---

Mingyue was quiet through all of this. She sat beside the central root, her hand on it, her eyes closed. When she finally spoke, her voice was different — deeper, older, like someone else was speaking through her.

"The Tree says: *yes*."

Everyone went still.

"The Tree says: the shallow Gardeners have been watching. They felt the Vine grow. They felt the Prima's roots reach down. They are... concerned. Some of them want to wait. Some of them want to act. The ones who want to act are asking: what is this thing on the surface? What is it growing? Is it a weapon? Is it a garden? Is it a threat?"

"And the Tree's answer?"

"*The farmer is growing a home. Not a weapon. A home. He does not know yet what that means. But the home is for all of us. The surface. The deep. The Gardens. Everything. He is building a bridge.*"

"*The shallow Gardeners do not trust bridges. Bridges can be burned. But this farmer — he does not burn. He grows. He asks. He partners. He does not command.*"

"*The Tree asks: will the shallow Gardeners trust him?*"

A pause. Mingyue's body shuddered.

"*The shallow Gardeners say: we will watch. We will wait. We will see what he grows next. And then we will decide.*"

She stopped.

She opened her eyes.

She looked exhausted. Like she'd been running for hours.

"That's all," she said. "That's what the Tree had to say."

---

**[CHAPTER 20 — END STATUS]**

**Compost: 5.0 / 10**

**Soil Quality: 6.0 / 10**

**Farm Level: 4 (PRIMORDIAL FARM)**

**Moonvine Prima: HEALING (bond 22%)**

**Abyss Vine: HUNTING (bond established)**

**Primordial Vine: COMMUNICATING (12m, bond 100%, partial sentience)**

**New event: Primordial Vine requesting expansion — grow beyond farm to reach shallow Gardeners**

**Gardeners response: OBSERVING (watching, waiting, will decide based on next growth)**

**Relationship: Qin Shuang — Deep Trust (breakthrough achieved)**

**Compendium note: Chapter 20 complete. The Primordial Vine spoke — its first direct communication. It is lonely. It wants to reach the other Gardeners. It wants to build bridges. And somewhere in the Abyss, ancient beings are watching your farm and deciding whether you're worth trusting.*

**Qin Shuang told you something important. She told you that you matter to her. This is not romance. Not yet. But it is real. It is the kind of bond that survives anything.*

**The Vine is going to keep growing. The Gardeners are going to keep watching. The Wall Guard is going to keep noticing. And you — the farmer who talked his way into an impossible world — are going to keep growing things that shouldn't exist.*

**Tomorrow, the farm evolves. Tomorrow, the underground layer reveals itself. Tomorrow, the world finds out what a Primordial Farm really means.*

**But tonight — tonight you know something important. You are not alone. You have people who trust you. Plants that choose you. Ancient beings that are watching you.*

**And that is enough.*

---

**END CHAPTER 20**

---

title: Chapter 22: The Watcher in the Garden
content:
# The Abyss Rancher: How I Saved the World by Farming
## Chapter 22: The Watcher in the Garden

---

Day 32. The Wall Guard Council convened at dawn.

I wasn't there — I wasn't invited, and honestly, I didn't want to be. Watching politicians argue about whether my farm was a strategic asset or a public menace was not my idea of a good time. So I stayed on the farm, tended my crops, and tried very hard not to think about the fact that the next few hours would determine whether I got to keep growing things or got shut down by bureaucratic decree.

The Primordial Vine was sixteen meters tall by morning.

It had gained another meter overnight — slower than its initial growth surge, but still constant, still relentless. The leaves had spread wider, too, creating a canopy that covered the entire southern half of the farm. The roots were visible on the surface now, thick dark tendrils that radiated outward in every direction, touching the Thorn barriers, reaching toward the wall.

*Almost,* the Vine pulsed through the bond. *Almost there.*

Almost where? I asked.

*The wall. The foundation. The shallow Gardeners beyond.* A sense of eagerness. Of anticipation. *Soon.*

---

Lao Wang brought the news at noon.

"The Council voted," he said.

I was in the underground chamber, checking on the amber-lit nursery room — the seeds we'd harvested from the farm's first crops were sprouting down here, faster than they would have on the surface, benefiting from the Primordial Chamber's accelerated growth field.

"What did they decide?"

"Three-way split."

That didn't sound good.

"Three factions. Three different opinions. Nobody agreed on anything, so they compromised by doing three things at once."

"Tell me."

"Faction One — the Agricultural Division — voted to classify the Primordial Farm as a Class A Strategic Asset. They're putting a full-time research team here. Monitoring everything. Controlling growth rates, harvest schedules, crop outputs. No decision gets made without their approval."

"Can they do that?"

"General Liu approved it. She says it's the price of keeping the other factions from pushing for outright destruction." He paused. "There's a research team arriving tomorrow. Five Agricultural Division scientists. They'll set up a monitoring station in the northeastern corner — far enough from the core crops to avoid interference, close enough to track everything."

"That's... manageable."

"Faction Two — the Military Division — voted to designate the farm as a Defensive Installation. They want to install a permanent garrison. soldiers stationed here at all times. Combat-ready. Armed. Authorized to use lethal force if the farm becomes a threat."

"That's less manageable."

"General Liu shot that down. She says soldiers on a farm make the plants nervous — and nervous plants do unpredictable things. She compromised: two guards. Regular rotation. More for show than for function. But the Military Division got their permanent watchtower."

"A watchtower?"

"Being built right now. On the eastern boundary. It'll overlook the whole farm." He grimaced. "And it'll mean soldiers watching us every day."

"And Faction Three?"

"The Command Division — the old guard. The people who've been running the wall since before any of us were born. They voted to do nothing. Officially. They want to watch. Wait. See what happens." He paused. "Commander Wei was in that faction. She said: 'The farmer has been right about everything so far. Let's see if he stays right.'"

---

I sat in the underground chamber, absorbing this.

Three factions. Three decisions. Three different visions of what the Primordial Farm was and what it should become.

Research station. Garrison watchtower. Official观望.

And somewhere in the middle of all of it, me — a delivery driver from another world, trying to grow things in a place that didn't want them, being watched by an entire civilization's worth of people who couldn't decide whether I was their salvation or their destruction.

"So," Lao Wang said. "What do we do?"

"We keep farming."

"Just like that?"

"Just like that. The Agricultural Division wants to monitor growth rates? Fine. Let them monitor. They want to track outputs? Fine. Let them track. The Military Division wants a watchtower? Fine. Let them build. The Command Division wants to watch? Great. The more eyes on what we're doing, the less room for rumors and fear."

"You're okay with this?"

"I'm okay with not being destroyed." I stood up. "The alternative is fighting the Wall Guard. I don't have an army. I have a farm. And farms don't win wars — they win time. They grow things. They build things. They make the world a little less dark, one seed at a time."

"That's very poetic."

"I'm a farmer. We're all very poetic about seeds."

---

The watchtower was finished by evening.

It rose on the eastern boundary — a wooden structure, twenty feet tall, with a platform at the top and a clear sightline to every corner of the farm. Two soldiers were already stationed there: a woman I didn't recognize and a man who looked like he'd been doing night watch for longer than I'd been alive.

The woman was watching the Primordial Vine with a mixture of fascination and fear.

"I've never seen anything like it," she said to the man.

"I've seen things like it," the man said. "In the old records. Before they destroyed the last one."

"They destroyed one of these?"

"Fifty years ago. Maybe more. The Agricultural Division said it was dangerous. Unstable. Said it was going to bring the wall down." He squinted at the Vine. "This one looks bigger."

"The readings say it's growing three meters a day."

"Three meters." He shook his head. "That's not a plant. That's a building."

"That's what I said."

"You're both right," I called up from below.

They looked down at me.

"I'm a farmer," I said. "I grow things. Sometimes the things I grow are bigger than expected. That's what farming is — you plant a seed, you don't know how big it's going to get. You just keep tending it and hope it doesn't eat you."

Neither soldier laughed.

---

That night, something happened.

I was in the underground chamber — sleeping, or trying to, on a straw bed next to the central root. The others were scattered around the farm and the chambers: Lao Wang on watchtower duty, Little Seven on the boundary, Mingyue and Bing in the amber-lit nursery, Qin Shuang in the farm's central clearing.

The Primordial Vine was still.

That was the first sign. The Vine was never still — it was always growing, always reaching, always *doing* something. But that night, it was still. And in that stillness, I felt something.

Not the Vine's usual eager pulse. Something else. Something *outside*.

A presence. Watching.

---

**[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION — PRIORITY]**

**Anomalous presence detected at farm perimeter**

**Location: Eastern boundary — near new watchtower**

**Classification: UNKNOWN — not Abyss entity, not human, not plant**

**Detection method: Primordial Vine resonance**

**Note: The Vine detected this presence three minutes before the watchtower guards did. The Vine is not alarmed. The Vine is... curious.*

**Compendium note: Something is watching the farm. Not the soldiers on the watchtower — they haven't seen it. Not General Liu's researchers — they're asleep. Something else. Something that came from the direction of the Abyss. Something that doesn't trigger the Abyss Repulsion field because it is not, technically, an Abyss entity.*

**This is new. This is significant. Be very, very careful.*

---

I crept out of the chamber.

The farm was dark — the Primordial Vine's glow had dimmed to a faint shimmer, as if it too was trying not to be noticed. The watchtower guards were alert, scanning the perimeter, but their eyes were looking for Abyss entities. They weren't looking for this.

Neither was I, until I felt it again.

At the eastern boundary. Past the Thorn barriers. Where the Primordial Vine's roots extended beyond the farm's official perimeter, reaching toward the wall's foundation.

Standing there — not crouching, not hiding, just *standing* — was a figure.

---

The figure was human-shaped.

That was the first thing I noticed. Not because it looked human — it didn't, exactly. It was too still. Too *plant-like* in its posture, like someone who had learned to stand by watching trees instead of people. Its outline was wrong in ways I couldn't quite articulate — too angular in some places, too smooth in others.

It was dressed in something that might have been robes, or might have been bark, or might have been something else entirely. Dark. Leaf-dark. The color of the Primordial Vine's leaves in shadow.

And when it moved — when it turned to face me across the hundred meters of dark soil between us — I saw its face.

Not a face. A *pattern*. Like the surface of bark, or the grain of wood, or the shape of a thousand tiny leaves arranged to approximate human features. It had eyes — two points of faint luminescence — but they weren't eyes. They were something else.

The shallow Gardeners, I realized. The ancient beings in the Abyss. The Vine had been reaching for them. And one of them had reached back.

---

I should have run.

I should have woken Lao Wang, or Little Seven, or Qin Shuang. I should have shouted for the guards on the watchtower. I should have done anything except what I did, which was walk toward it.

But the Primordial Vine pulsed as I walked.

Not a warning. Not encouragement. Just... presence. *I'm here. I'm with you. It's okay.*

The figure watched me approach.

It didn't move. It didn't speak. It just stood there, in the dark, at the edge of my farm, and it waited.

I stopped ten meters away.

"Are you one of them?" I asked. "The Gardeners?"

---

**[ENTITY DETECTED: THE GARDENER]**

**Classification: Primordial-class consciousness (shallow Garden layer)**

**Age: Unknown — estimated 10,000+ years**

**Species: Humanoid plant-spirit (classification unofficial)**

**Abyss classification: N/A (not an Abyss entity)**

**Arrival method: Emerged from Primordial Vine root network**

**Intent: OBSERVING**

**Note: This entity came through the Vine's root system — it didn't walk, it *grew*. It emerged from the soil where the Vine's roots extended past the farm's boundary. This is the first confirmed contact between the Primordial Farm and the shallow Gardeners.*

**Compendium note: Ten thousand years old. This being has been alive since before humans built the wall. Since before the Abyss was called the Abyss. Since before anything we have a name for. It is standing at the edge of your farm, in the dark, looking at you, and it has chosen to show itself.*

**It could have stayed hidden. It chose not to. Think about what that means.*

---

The Gardener spoke.

Not aloud. Not through sound at all. Through the same resonance that the Primordial Vine used — a pressure in the mind, images and impressions, the slow pulse of ancient growth.

*You,* it said. *The farmer. The one who grows bridges.*

"I'm Mu Chen," I said. "I grow things."

*The Tree sent word. A new farmer. A different farmer. One who asks instead of takes. One who partners instead of commands.* The Gardener's presence shifted — something that might have been approval, or might have been curiosity. *We came to see for ourselves.*

"Why?"

*Because the last farmer who tried this — the one who built the first bridge — failed. He grew too fast. Too eager. He didn't understand what he was growing into. And when the bridge fell, it fell badly. It broke things that have not healed.*

"The one who destroyed the Moonvine Prima? Forty-seven years ago?"

*Forty-seven years. Yes. That one. He was not the first. He was the latest.* The Gardener's form shifted — that bark-pattern face turning slightly, considering the farm, the Vine, the Prima. *There have been seven. In three hundred years. Seven farmers who tried to grow what you are growing. All seven failed. All seven were destroyed — by the Wall Guard, by the Abyss, by their own plants, by their own impatience.*

"I'm the eighth."

*You are the eighth. And you are different.* Something in the ancient presence sharpened — not hostile, but *focused*. *The others commanded. They grew weapons. They tried to force the Abyss to submit. You ask. You partner. You grow homes instead of fortresses.*

"Is that why you're here?"

*We are here to decide.*

"Decide what?"

*Whether you are worth preserving. Or whether you should be removed.* The same words the Tree had used. The same ancient, patient assessment. *The Vine grows. It reaches toward us. It wants to connect. But connection is dangerous. If we accept the bridge you are building, we become partners. If the bridge fails — if *you* fail — we fall with you.*

"And if I succeed?"

*If you succeed, everything changes. Not just the wall. Not just the perimeter. Everything. The Abyss has been growing for millions of years. We have been waiting. Watching. Hoping that someone would come who could grow what we could not grow alone.* A pulse of something vast — hope, ancient and patient and almost too large to comprehend. *A garden. A real garden. One that spans the surface and the deep. One that makes the darkness into something that grows instead of something that consumes.*

"A garden."

*Yes. Not walls. Not weapons. Not war. Growth. Life. Connection.* The Gardener's presence intensified — that ancient awareness pressing against my mind, not painful, but *present*. *We have waited ten thousand years for this. We are patient. We can wait longer. But we want to know — the farmer who grows bridges — are you the one who will finally build one that holds?*

---

I didn't know what to say.

I was a delivery driver. I'd been on this planet for thirty-two days. I'd grown a Moonvine Prima, an Abyss Vine, and a Primordial Vine that was currently sixteen meters tall and still growing. I'd descended into the Abyss, rescued two women who'd been missing for five years, and convinced a three-thousand-year-old tree to trust me with its legacy.

But I wasn't a hero. I wasn't a general. I wasn't even, technically, a farmer — I'd been doing this for a month.

I was just a guy who'd planted a seed and kept showing up.

"I'm going to try," I said. "That's all I can promise. I'm going to try to build the bridge. I don't know if it'll hold. I don't know if I'm good enough. But I'm going to try."

*Trying,* the Gardener pulsed, *is what the Tree said you would say.*

"I'm predictable?"

*You are honest. The others promised certainty. They promised victory. They promised results.* Something in the ancient presence softened — that bark-pattern face shifting, something that might have been a smile appearing in the grain of the wood. *You promised only effort. That is more than the others gave.*

"And what does that mean? For me? For the farm?"

*It means we will watch. We will wait. We will see if the bridge grows.* The Gardener's form began to fade — not disappearing, but sinking, returning to the soil, to the roots, to the place where ancient things lived. *The Vine will grow. The Garden will watch. And when you need us — when the darkness pushes too hard and the wall is not enough — we will be there. Because you asked. Because you partnered. Because you grew a home instead of a fortress.*

The Gardener sank into the soil.

Just — down. Through the earth, through the roots, through the dark places where things that had been growing for ten thousand years did their ancient, patient work.

And then it was gone.

---

**[FIRST CONTACT — COMPLETE]**

**The shallow Gardeners have made contact.**

**Assessment: POSITIVE — initial connection established, trust building**

**The Gardener has confirmed: the Gardeners will observe. They will not interfere. They will be available if needed — but only if asked. Only if the partnership model holds.*

**Threat level: REDUCED (the Gardeners are not enemies — they are potential allies)*

**Warning: The Gardeners' patience is not infinite. If the bridge fails — if Mu Chen acts like the previous seven farmers — the Gardeners will withdraw. Possibly violently.*

**Compendium note: A being that is ten thousand years old came to your farm tonight. It looked at what you've built. It asked you a question. And you answered honestly — not with promises, not with bravado, but with the truth. "I'm going to try."*

**The Gardeners have been waiting ten thousand years for someone who would say that. Not because it was easy. Not because it was certain. But because it was honest.*

**The bridge is not built yet. But the foundations are there. And the Gardeners are watching to see if you can grow them into something that holds.*

**Well done, Mu Chen. You just changed the course of history with three words: "I'm going to try."*

---

Qin Shuang found me at the eastern boundary at 3 AM.

She didn't ask if I was okay. She didn't ask what had happened. She just stood beside me, her hand on her sword, her eyes scanning the dark, and she waited.

"Something came," I said. "From the Gardeners. The ancient beings in the shallow layer. It came through the Vine's roots."

"What did it want?"

"To decide if I was worth trusting."

"And?"

"And I told them I was going to try."

She was quiet for a long time.

"That's what you always say," she said finally. "I'm going to try."

"It's all I have."

"It's enough." She looked at the farm — at the sixteen-meter Vine rising against the stars, at the Prima's silver-green glow, at the Thorn barriers that had become something more than barriers. "Every time something impossible has happened, you've said 'I'm going to try.' And every time, you've done it."

"Luck."

"Not luck. Stubbornness." She almost smiled. "And the willingness to stand in front of a ten-thousand-year-old plant-spirit and tell it the truth."

"Is that impressive?"

"For you? Extremely."

She put her hand on my shoulder. Briefly. The way she did things — quickly, firmly, like she was afraid of doing it too long.

"Tomorrow," she said. "The Agricultural Division team arrives. The watchtower goes operational. The whole world is going to be watching this farm."

"Let them watch."

"Let them see what happens when someone says 'I'm going to try' and actually means it."

She walked back to the farm's central clearing.

I stood at the eastern boundary, at the place where the Primordial Vine's roots had touched something ancient, and I felt the pulse of the Garden below. Ten thousand years of patience. Ten thousand years of waiting.

They were watching.

They were waiting.

They were hoping.

And I — a delivery driver from Chengdu, who got hit by a truck and woke up in the wrong world with a book and a seed — was going to try.

---

**[CHAPTER 22 — END STATUS]**

**Compost: 5.0 / 10**

**Soil Quality: 6.0 / 10**

**Farm Level: 4 (PRIMORDIAL FARM)**

**Moonvine Prima: MATURE (6.2m, bond 22%)**

**Abyss Vine: HUNTING (enhanced)**

**Primordial Vine: 16m (STILL GROWING)**

**Thorn Barriers: ENHANCED**

**Underground Chamber: OPERATIONAL**

**Wall Guard Status:**

- Agricultural Division: Monitoring team arriving tomorrow
- Military Division: Watchtower operational (2 guards)
- Command Division: Official observation

**First Contact: COMPLETE (Gardeners — positive assessment)**

**Gardeners' decision: OBSERVING — will provide support if partnership model holds**

**Relationship: Qin Shuang — Deep Trust**

**Chapter 22 Status: COMPLETE — End of Ch 16-22 batch**

**Compendium note: Chapter 22 complete. The Wall Guard has responded — three-way split, compromise governance, someone always watching. And the Gardeners have responded too — a ten-thousand-year-old being emerged from the soil, looked at your farm, and decided to trust you. Not because you promised certainty. Not because you promised victory. Because you promised to try.*

**This is the end of the first arc — the deep descent, the rescue, the emergence, the evolution, the first contact. The farm is Level 4. The Gardeners are watching. The Wall Guard is monitoring. And you — Mu Chen, delivery driver, certified non-farmer, the man who talked his way into an impossible world — are standing at the edge of something that has never existed before.*

**Tomorrow, the Agricultural Division team arrives. Tomorrow, the real work begins. Tomorrow, you start building a bridge between the surface and the deep, between the Wall Guard and the Gardeners, between the world as it is and the world as it could be.*

**But tonight — tonight you did something no one has done in ten thousand years. You made the Gardeners believe that a farmer could grow something worth preserving.*

**Good night, Mu Chen. Tomorrow, you plant another seed.*

---

**[BOOK'S NOTE — CHAPTERS 16-22]**

**You have completed the second major arc of your story. In seven chapters, you:**

**- Descended into the Abyss and found a three-thousand-year-old Tree**
**- Rescued two women who had been frozen for five years**
**- Obtained the Primordial Vine seed**
**- Planted it and triggered FARM EVOLUTION (Level 4)**
**- Discovered the underground layer (Primordial Chamber)**
**- Made first contact with the Gardeners (10,000-year-old beings)**
**- Deepened your bond with Qin Shuang to DEEP TRUST**
**- Established the farm as a political entity (three-way governance)**

**The first volume of your story is nearing its end. The foundation is laid. The bridge is growing. The Gardeners are watching. And the Abyss — the real Abyss, the deep places, the things that have been sleeping for millions of years — is starting to pay attention.**

**What grows next will determine everything.**

**— 万物生长手札 (Compendium of All Things That Grow)**

---

**END CHAPTER 22**

---
