title: Chapter 26: The Farmer Who Grew War
content:
# The Abyss Rancher: How I Saved the World by Farming
## Chapter 26: The Farmer Who Grew War

---

Day 36. Hour 4. The Dread Wraith arrived.

It came out of the darkness like a hole in reality.

That's the only way I can describe it. One moment, the eastern horizon was black — Abyss-black, the color of a sky that had never seen stars. The next moment, there was a *shape* in that blackness. A shape that was darker than the darkness around it. A shape that moved through the night like smoke made of malice.

The soldiers saw it and went still.

Not frozen — still. The kind of stillness that comes when your brain recognizes something so far outside your experience that it doesn't know how to react. Lao Wang lowered his crossbow. Little Seven stopped moving. Even Qin Shuang — who'd faced Hollow Stalkers and Abyss Crawlers and things that had no names — stood transfixed.

I saw it too.

Eight meters tall. Maybe ten. Built like a nightmare — elongated, wrong, *twisted*. Not a body so much as a *presence* given form. It had arms, but they were too long, too jointed, bending in directions arms shouldn't bend. It had a head — if you could call it that — a shape like a hollow skull with no eyes, just two points of light where eyes should be, burning with a cold intelligence that looked at my farm and saw *food*.

It was surrounded by a cloud of smaller entities — Shadow Wraiths, Class 3, too many to count. But they were incidental. Supporting cast. The main event was the Dread Wraith itself, and it was walking toward my farm with the unhurried confidence of something that had never been stopped.

The Vine pulsed through my bond: *It comes.*

"I see it."

*It killed eleven Gardeners the last time. Took three seasons for the Garden to replace what it consumed. It is not just strong. It is hollow. It feeds on life. It feeds on growth. It feeds on everything green and growing and alive.*

"And you think it wants to eat my farm."

*It wants to eat everything.*

---

I had maybe sixty seconds before it reached the Thorn Barriers.

The Thorn Barriers. Four-and-a-half meters of enhanced, toxic vegetation. What would they do against a Class 5 entity?

"Fire Flowers!" I screamed. "Eastern point — full spread! Now!"

The ten remaining Fire Flowers erupted simultaneously.

Ten spore clouds, concentrated on the approaching Dread Wraith. Ten streams of pure toxic compound, fired at maximum pressure, aimed at the center of that nightmare silhouette. The mist should have killed anything with an Abyss signature. It had killed Hollow Stalkers. It had annihilated goblins.

The Dread Wraith walked through it.

Not slowed. Not hurt. Not even *paused*. It walked through the spore cloud the way a mountain walks through fog — indifferent, unstoppable, towering. The toxic compound touched its form and evaporated. The poison that killed everything else dissolved against whatever that thing was made of.

"What the hell—" Little Seven said.

"It's not working!"

"Obviously!"

The Dread Wraith reached the Thorn Barriers.

It didn't climb. It didn't push. It just — *touched* the barrier. One elongated arm, extended, fingers brushing the Thorn Wall's thickest section.

And the Thorn Wall *died*.

Not burned. Not crushed. *Died*. The thorns withered. The stems blackened. The root network — that vast underground web of growth and life — rotted from the inside out. A section of barrier ten meters wide simply ceased to exist, collapsing into a pile of dead, gray ash.

"One barrier down," I whispered.

*It feeds on growth,* the Vine repeated. *Anything alive — anything green — it consumes. That is what a Dread Wraith is. A hole in the garden of the world.*

"How do I kill it?"

*I don't know. No one has ever killed one.*

---

The Dread Wraith stepped through the gap in the barriers and kept walking.

Behind it, the Shadow Wraiths poured through — hundreds of them, filling the space the Class 5 had created. The soldiers opened fire, but Shadow Wraiths were faster than Hollow Stalkers, smarter, and they moved in swarms. Crossbow bolts took down ten; a hundred more came through.

"Second line!" Qin Shuang's voice cut through the chaos. "Second line — hold!"

The second line engaged. Swords flashing. Soldiers screaming. The clash was brutal and immediate — bodies falling on both sides, blood (red and black) painting the dark soil.

But I wasn't watching the Shadow Wraiths. I was watching the Dread Wraith.

It was walking toward the center of the farm. Toward the Vine. Toward *me*.

Every living thing it passed died. The Fire Flowers in its path withered. The grass beneath its feet turned brown and cracked. The air around it smelled like rot and ash — the smell of something that consumed life as it moved.

*Mu Chen,* the Vine pulsed. *I will hold it.*

"You'll die."

*Maybe. But I will hold it. Go to the underground chamber. The healing roots. Let them fix you while you can.*

"I'm not leaving you to fight alone—"

*You are the farmer. I am the Vine. The Vine protects the farm. That is what I am. That is what I was grown to be.* The Vine's presence was fierce now — not just ancient patience but something sharper. Something *warlike*. *I did not grow for three thousand years to be eaten without fighting back.*

The Dread Wraith was thirty meters away.

Twenty.

Fifteen.

I ran.

---

The underground chamber saved my life.

Not because of its walls — though the stone helped — but because of the roots. The Primordial Chamber's root network was a living, healing environment. The moment I dropped through the trapdoor, the roots wrapped around my body, and I felt the regenerative pulse that the Vine had used to heal the Prima. Ancient nutrients. Primordial growth energy. Life, distilled and concentrated and pressed into my skin like a poultice made of centuries.

My exhaustion faded. My aching muscles relaxed. The bruises from the battle faded like shadows in sunlight.

But I could still feel the Vine.

Through the bond. Through the roots. Through the connection that had been growing since the moment I planted that impossible seed. I felt the Vine's trunk — twenty-six meters of ancient growth — bracing itself. I felt the lateral roots rising to the surface, thickening, hardening into something more than roots. I felt the secondary vines extending, multiplying, creating a web of vegetation that had never existed before.

The Vine was *changing*.

It was growing weapons.

*You will not leave,* the Vine pulsed. Not a question. A statement. A declaration. *You will watch. You will learn. And then — when I fall — you will grow something that finishes what I started.*

"I'm not letting you fall."

*That is not your choice. But I appreciate the sentiment.*

---

I watched through the bond.

I couldn't see the battle from the underground chamber — the walls blocked my sight — but I could *feel* it. The Vine's senses were my senses. The Vine's experience was my experience. And what I felt was —

*Battle*.

The Dread Wraith's arm swept through the Vine's primary trunk, and the trunk *bent* — didn't break, didn't wither, just bent, its ancient wood flexing like a living thing. The Vine responded by launching a volley of root-tendrils from below ground, wrapping the Dread Wraith's legs, anchoring itself in place.

The Dread Wraith tried to consume the roots. It *did* consume some of them — blackened, withered, dead. But for every root it ate, two more grew. The Vine was feeding it — deliberately — not to weaken itself but to *slow* it, to trap it, to hold it in place.

*Fighting*, the Vine pulsed. *I remember fighting. It has been so long. The Garden remembers. The Tree remembers. I remember.*

A volley of secondary vines — thick as my arm, studded with thorns that dripped with concentrated toxic compound — erupted from the Vine's main body and struck the Dread Wraith's torso. The thorns embedded in its form. The toxin flooded into it.

The Dread Wraith *flinched*.

For the first time since it appeared, the Class 5 entity showed a sign of pain.

It is hurt, I realized. The toxins don't kill it, but they *hurt* it. They're poisoning it. Slowly. Relentlessly. The way I killed the goblins — the Vine was going to kill the Dread Wraith the same way. Thorn by thorn. Root by root. Growing faster than it could consume. Wearing it down. Outlasting it.

*The Gardeners,* the Vine pulsed. *They taught the Tree. The Tree taught me. You do not kill the darkness with fire. You kill it with growth. You grow so fast, so vast, so *relentlessly alive* that the darkness chokes on you.*

---

The battle raged for six hours.

I stayed in the underground chamber, feeding the Vine through the bond, pouring everything I had into its roots. The chamber's regenerative energy flowed upward — not healing me anymore, but channeling through me, amplifying the Vine's growth. The Compost reserves depleted. The Soil Quality dropped. But the Vine kept growing. Kept fighting. Kept *enduring*.

On the surface, Qin Shuang and her soldiers held the second line against the Shadow Wraiths. They lost two more soldiers — killed, not wounded — and another five were injured badly enough to be evacuated to Dr. Lin's field medical station. But they held.

The Thorn Barriers regenerated — slowly, painfully, but they regenerated. The Vine's roots reached down, pulled nutrients from the deep soil, and pushed new growth through the dead sections. New thorns emerged. New stems. New toxic leaves.

The Dread Wraith was still fighting, but it was slowing.

Every time it consumed a piece of the Vine, two more pieces grew. Every time it struck, the Vine bent and反弹. Every time it tried to advance, the roots caught its legs, the thorns slowed its arms, the toxins ate at its form.

By hour six, the Dread Wraith had consumed forty percent of the Vine's visible mass.

By hour seven, the Vine had regenerated sixty percent.

The Dread Wraith was dying. Not fast. Not dramatically. But dying — being outgrown, outpaced, outlasted by something that had been growing in the dark long before the Abyss existed.

And then — at hour seven and thirteen minutes — the Dread Wraith made a mistake.

It reached for the Moonvine Prima.

The Prima was a three-hundred-year-old Primordial organism. It was bonded to me at twenty-two percent. It was part of this farm, part of this family, part of *my* responsibility. And when the Dread Wraith's grasping fingers reached for its silver-green glow —

The Prima *attacked*.

Not with thorns. Not with toxins. With *light*.

---

The Moonvine Prima's moonlight was different from the Vine's growth-light.

The Vine's light was life — warm, organic, the light of growing things. The Prima's light was *cold*. Silver and pure and utterly without warmth. It fell on the Dread Wraith like water on a grease fire — hissing, sizzling, evaporating.

The Dread Wraith recoiled.

It had touched the Prima's light for perhaps two seconds. In those two seconds, a section of its form — the arm that had reached for the plant — simply *ceased to be*. Not burned. Not consumed. Erased. Like a shadow exposed to sunlight.

The Dread Wraith screamed.

That sound — the same breath-like noise I'd heard before, but amplified, distorted, *agonized*. It stumbled backward, away from the Prima, away from the farm, away from the light that had hurt it.

And the Vine pressed forward.

Every root it had. Every vine. Every tendril of growth and life and ancient, patient persistence. It wrapped the Dread Wraith in a cocoon of vegetation and *squeezed*.

---

**[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION — HOUR 7]**

**Dread Wraith: CRITICAL DAMAGE**

**Entity Status: DYING**

**The Vine has the Dread Wraith in a full growth-cocoon. The Prima's moonlight is burning through its form. The toxins are systemic. It will not survive another hour.*

**Soldiers Status:**
- Killed: 2
- Injured: 13 (4 serious)
- Combat-effective: 15

**Farm Status:**
- Thorn Barriers: 40% remaining (regenerating)
- Fire Flowers: 5/15 remaining (spores depleted)
- Primordial Vine: DAMAGED (45% mass lost, regrowing rapidly)
- Moonvine Prima: ACTIVE (moonlight attack confirmed effective)
- Compost: 4.8 → 3.2 / 10 (massive resource drain)
- Soil Quality: 5.7 → 4.9 / 10

**Assessment: You won. Not through force. Not through firepower. Through growth. You grew a plant that was too stubborn to die, too vast to consume, and too patient to stop. The Dread Wraith — Class 5, sixty years of waiting — is dying on your farm. Killed by a farmer, a Vine, and a Prima.*

**This is going to be in every military textbook for the next hundred years.*

---

The Dread Wraith died at hour seven and forty-one minutes.

It didn't explode. Didn't dissolve dramatically. It simply — faded. The Vine's roots loosened their grip, and the Dread Wraith's form began to come apart. Piece by piece. Fragment by fragment. Like ash carried on the wind, it dispersed, dissipated, and was gone.

Where it had stood, there was only a dark stain on the soil — scorched earth, the color of old blood, smelling of rot and absence.

And the Vine, battered and scarred but *standing*, pulsing through the bond with a feeling I could only describe as exhausted satisfaction.

*It is done,* the Vine said. *The hollow one is gone.*

"You did it."

*We did it. The Prima helped. The roots helped. The soil helped.* A pulse of warmth — affection, maybe, or gratitude. *You helped.*

"I just planted you."

*Yes. And I grew. That is the nature of farming. You plant. Things grow. Sometimes what grows is bigger than anyone expected.*

---

I climbed out of the underground chamber at hour eight.

The farm was a disaster zone.

The eastern boundary was half-destroyed — Thorn Barriers collapsed, soil scorched, the ground carpeted with dead entities and dying plants. The Shadow Wraith corpses numbered in the hundreds. The bodies of two soldiers lay on the second line, covered with blankets, waiting to be carried to the Wall Guard's memorial.

But the farm was still standing.

The Vine was still growing — already regenerating new thorns, new stems, new life. The Prima's silver-green glow was brighter than ever, its moonlight攻击 having energized it in ways I didn't fully understand. And in the gaps between the destruction, new growth was already emerging. Green shoots pushing through the ash. Roots extending into the damaged soil. Life, refusing to stop.

Qin Shuang found me standing at the eastern boundary, staring at the Dread Wraith's final stain.

"You're alive," she said.

"So are you."

"So is your farm."

"So are my plants." I looked at her. She had a cut across her forehead, a bandaged arm, and the most exhausted expression I'd ever seen on a human face. "How many did we lose?"

"Two. Corporal Huang and Private Liang. Good soldiers. They died holding the second line." Her voice was steady, controlled. The soldier's mask, firmly in place. "Eight more are wounded. Four won't fight again."

"I'm sorry."

"They knew the risks. They volunteered." She paused. "Just like you."

I didn't have an answer for that.

We stood together, looking at the Vine — battered but alive, scarred but growing, holding the farm together with roots and determination and the sheer stubbornness of life.

"The Vine saved us," I said. "It grew weapons. Defenses. It changed into something that could fight a Class 5 entity and win."

"I saw."

"It spoke to me. Through the bond. It told me it was grown to protect farms. To protect farmers. That's what it was designed for — three thousand years ago, by the Gardeners, before anyone remembered what farming was."

Qin Shuang was quiet for a moment.

Then: "Can it do it again? If another wave comes?"

"I don't know. The Compost is almost gone. The Soil Quality is shot. It's damaged." I looked at the Vine's main trunk — a quarter of its mass missing, slowly regrowing, pulsing with exhausted vitality. "But if another wave comes, it will try. That's what plants do. They try."

"That's a terrible plan."

"It's the only plan I have."

---

**[CHAPTER 26 — END STATUS]**

**Day: 36 (Hours into Tide: 8)**

**Farm Level: 4 (PRIMORDIAL FARM)**

**Primordial Vine: 26m → 18m (DAMAGED, 45% mass lost, regenerating)**

**Moonvine Prima: 6.2m (ENHANCED — moonlight attack unlocked)**

**Thorn Barriers: 40% (regenerating)**

**Fire Flowers: 5/15 (combat-effective)**

**Compost: 3.2 / 10 (LOW — critical shortage)**

**Soil Quality: 4.9 / 10 (contaminated, needs remediation)**

**Human Casualties:**
- Killed: 2 (Corporal Huang, Private Liang)
- Seriously wounded: 4 (permanent)
- Moderately wounded: 5
- Combat-effective: 15

**Entities Killed:**
- Goblin-rank: ~900
- Hollow Stalkers: ~300
- Shadow Wraiths: ~400
- **Dread Wraith: 1 (CLASS 5 — FIRST KILL)**

**Relationship: Qin Shuang — Deep Trust**

**Chapter 26 Status: COMPLETE**

**Compendium note: A Class 5 Dread Wraith came to your farm. It killed two soldiers. It destroyed forty percent of your barriers. It ate forty-five percent of your Vine. And then it died. Killed not by firepower or military might, but by a plant that grew faster than it could eat and a Moonvine Prima that burned its arm off with moonlight. The Vine changed in battle — unlocked combat abilities, defensive growth patterns, something ancient and warlike that had been sleeping inside it for three thousand years. You held the line. You survived the worst thing the Abyss could throw at you. And the Vine whispered, as it collapsed into exhausted sleep: "I did not grow for three thousand years to be eaten without fighting back."*

**Chapter 27 preview: But the Tide isn't over. The Dread Wraith was the second wave. The third wave — the real wave, the one the Vine has been dreading — is coming. Something even bigger. Something from the third layer. Something that makes the Dread Wraith look like a warm-up act. And Qin Shuang receives a message that will change everything: her sister has been spotted. In the outer settlements. Near the third-layer entrance. Alone.*

**Next chapter: "The Wraith was just the appetizer."**

---

**END CHAPTER 26**

title: Chapter 29: What We Call Ourselves
content:
# The Abyss Rancher: How I Saved the World by Farming
## Chapter 29: What We Call Ourselves

---

Day 41. The farm was quiet.

Not the tense quiet of pre-battle waiting. The *real* quiet — the kind that comes after a storm, when everything is broken and everyone is too tired to speak. The kind of quiet that lets you finally hear yourself think.

I'd been awake for an hour, walking the farm's perimeter, taking inventory of the damage. The eastern boundary was still a mess — scorched barriers, empty Fire Flower pots, soil that would need months to recover. But everywhere I looked, new growth was emerging. Green shoots through the ash. Roots extending into the damaged earth. Life, stubborn and relentless, refusing to be defeated.

The Vine was thirty meters tall now.

I stood at its base, looking up at the canopy that was slowly taking shape — a roof of dark-purple leaves spreading over the farm's eastern half, thick enough to provide shade, dense enough to offer cover. It was the Vine's architecture. Its design. Its vision of what this farm was supposed to become.

*Good morning, Farmer.*

"Morning. You're growing fast."

*Always. Especially after battle. The Tide pushed me. I grew to meet the pressure.* A pulse of satisfaction — ancient, patient, content. *The canopy will be complete in three days. Then the farm will have its first permanent roof.*

"A roof."

*A shelter. For the plants. For the people. For whatever grows next.* The Vine's presence was warm. *You dreamed last night.*

"How did you know?"

*I felt it. The Gardeners reached through me. They showed you the Seed.*

"They did."

*And?*

"I don't know yet. I'm a farmer. I grow things. But this — the Seed, the Bridge, the whole thing — it feels bigger than me."

*Everything big starts small. A seed is very small. And then it grows.*

"You're very philosophical for a plant."

*I have had three thousand years to think about growth. I have come to understand that size is irrelevant. What matters is direction. What matters is the choice to keep going, even when the soil is poisoned and the sky is wrong and everything seems impossible.*

"When did you get so wise?"

*I learned from a farmer who talked to his plants every day. Who treated us as partners, not tools. Who asked instead of commanded.* A pulse of warmth. *You.*

---

The knock at the farm gate came at 9 AM.

I was in the underground chamber, checking on the compost levels and the Prima's root health, when Lao Wang's voice echoed down: "Mu Chen! Someone's coming!"

I climbed the ladder and walked to the gate.

Qin Shuang was already there.

She was standing perfectly still, her hands at her sides, her body rigid with an emotion I couldn't name. Her eyes were fixed on the road — the dirt path that led from the wall's main gate to the outer perimeter — and in her expression, I saw something I'd never seen before.

Hope. Pure, terrified, overwhelming hope.

"She's coming," Qin Shuang whispered.

"Who—"

Movement. A figure on the road. Small, distant, moving slowly — too slowly for someone walking under their own power. But walking nonetheless. One step at a time. Through the ash. Through the evidence of the Tide. Through the destruction that had consumed everything else.

Through the darkness toward the light.

Qin Yue arrived at the farm gate twenty minutes later.

---

She was twenty-three. Three years younger than Qin Shuang, with the same gray eyes and the same dark hair and a thinness that spoke of weeks — months — of deprivation. She was wearing torn clothes that might once have been the uniform of an outer-settlement militia. She had a wound on her left arm, bandaged with strips of cloth that had gone dark with old blood. She was carrying something in her hands — cradling it, protecting it, holding it like it was the most precious thing in the world.

And in her hands, a seed glowed.

Colors I didn't have names for. Light that had no source. Pulsing with the same rhythm as the Vine, the same ancient patience, the same impossible life.

The Seed of the Second Garden.

"Shuang," Qin Yue said. Her voice was hoarse, exhausted, barely above a whisper.

Qin Shuang moved.

She crossed the distance between them in three strides, grabbed her sister, and pulled her into the tightest embrace I've ever seen. Qin Yue dropped the Seed — gently, carefully, into the grass at their feet — and collapsed into her sister's arms. The two of them sank to the ground together, holding each other, and for a long moment there was no sound except the quiet, broken sobs of someone who'd finally, finally come home.

I looked away. Gave them privacy. Picked up the Seed.

---

The Seed was warm in my hands.

Not hot — warm. Like holding a living thing. Like holding a heartbeat. The light inside it pulsed slowly, steadily, and through the bond — yes, I had a bond with it already, the moment I touched it — I felt the Vine's presence surge with recognition.

*It is here,* the Vine pulsed. *The other half of me. The Seed of the Second Garden. We were one, once. Before the old farmer scattered us. We were one plant, grown across two layers, and when he failed, we were broken apart.*

"What are you supposed to do?"

*Grow. Connect. Build.* The Vine's presence was dense with ancient longing. *The Seed is me. I am the Seed. We are two halves of the same thing — a Bridge spanning the Abyss and the surface. Alone, we are incomplete. Together...*

"Together?"

*Together, we grow the Second Garden. The real one. The one the Gardeners have been waiting twelve hundred years for.*

---

The sisters recovered in the farm's central clearing.

Dr. Lin's team had set up a field medical station after the Tide — cots, supplies, basic care for the walking wounded. Qin Yue was on one of those cots now, wrapped in a blanket, a cup of hot broth in her hands, being examined by Dr. Lin with the focused intensity of a scientist encountering a new species.

"The wound is superficial," Dr. Lin said. "Three days old, minimum. It's healing, but slowly — she hasn't had proper nutrition in weeks. Possibly months."

"I'm fine," Qin Yue said. Her voice was stronger now — still hoarse, but steady. "I've survived worse."

"You've survived the third layer of the Abyss," Dr. Lin said. "Alone. With no support, no equipment, no backup. That is not 'fine.' That is extraordinary."

Qin Yue shrugged. It was a gesture I recognized — Qin Shuang did the same thing when someone complimented her. The deflection of someone who'd learned to be invisible.

"The Seed called to me," she said. "Three weeks ago. I was in the outer settlements, doing the usual — scouting, scavenging, surviving — and I felt it. A pulse. Like something was waking up deep underground. Like something was *calling*."

"How did you know where to go?" I asked.

"I didn't. Not at first. I just — followed the feeling. Walked toward the wall. Then past the wall. Into the first layer, then the second. It was..." She paused. Looked at the Seed, which I'd placed on a nearby table, still glowing softly. "It was like the Seed was guiding me. Showing me the path. Leading me to where it had fallen."

"The Gardeners said the seventh farmer scattered it. Twelve hundred years ago."

"They didn't scatter it randomly." Qin Yue's gray eyes — Qin Shuang's gray eyes — fixed on me with an intensity that was almost uncomfortable. "They scattered it *specifically*. Across the three layers. One piece in each layer, connected by roots that grew through the rock itself. They were waiting for someone to find them. To put them back together."

"And you found all three pieces?"

"No. Just this one. The piece in the third layer." She paused. "The Seed was in the third layer. The deepest place. The place where the Gardeners grew, before the Abyss swallowed everything. I had to go down. Past the second layer. Past the things that live in the deep. Past the darkness that no one has ever survived."

"Why?" Qin Shuang's voice was sharp. "Why would you do that? You could have died. You *should* have died."

"I know." Qin Yue looked at her sister. "But I felt it, Shuang. When I touched the Seed. I felt what it was supposed to do. I felt the Bridge — the connection it was supposed to create between the Abyss and the surface. And I felt that it was *close*. That it was almost ready. That all it needed was someone to find the pieces and put them together."

"And you volunteered?"

"Who else was going to do it?"

Qin Shuang stood up sharply. "I was! I was going to get you out. I had a plan. I was working on it—"

"Shuang." Qin Yue stood too, swaying slightly, steadying herself on the cot. "I love you. I know you've been fighting to get me inside the wall. I know you've sacrificed everything. But this — the Seed, the Bridge — this is bigger than the wall. Bigger than the settlements. Bigger than anything we've been fighting for."

"How do you know?"

"Because the Seed told me." Qin Yue picked up the Seed from the table. Held it. The light inside pulsed brighter — as if responding to her touch. "It showed me what it's supposed to grow into. A garden that spans both worlds. A living pathway. A connection that turns the Abyss from a threat into a habitat. From a darkness into a place where things can grow."

"That's impossible."

"So is killing a Behemoth with flowers." Qin Yue smiled — tired, fragile, but real. "But he did it."

---

**[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION — SEED DISCOVERY]**

**THE SEED OF THE SECOND GARDEN**

**Classification: Primordial-class cultivation resource**

**Origin: Original Bridge-plant, grown 12,000 years ago, scattered 1,200 years ago**

**Current status: ONE OF THREE PIECES RECOVERED (third layer piece)**

**Function: When combined with the Primordial Vine (surface piece), can trigger the growth of the SECOND GARDEN — a living Bridge spanning the Abyss and the surface*

**Warning: The Seed is dormant. It must be planted by a bonded farmer. The bonding process will be intense — possibly dangerous. The seventh farmer died attempting this. The eighth farmer has a choice.*

**The Vine's assessment: *Ready. Whenever you are ready. I have waited three thousand years. I can wait a little longer. But I hope you are ready soon. The Bridge needs to grow. The darkness is waiting.*

**Compost: 2.5 / 10**

**Soil Quality: 5.1 / 10**

**Moonvine Prima bond: 30%**

---

I walked to the eastern boundary after the sisters' reunion.

The Vine was waiting. Thirty meters tall, its canopy spreading, its roots deep in the soil that was slowly healing from the Tide's destruction. The Seed rested in my pocket, still warm, still pulsing.

*You have been quiet,* the Vine said.

"I've been thinking."

*About the Seed.*

"About everything. About the seventh farmer. About what happens if I try this and fail. About what happens if I try this and *succeed*." I looked up at the Vine's canopy — that vast, spreading architecture of dark-purple leaves. "The seventh farmer tried to control it. The Gardeners said that's what went wrong. But what if I don't know how to do it differently? What if I'm just as wrong, in a different way?"

*You will not control the Seed. You will partner with it. Like you partnered with me. Like you partnered with the Prima.*

"And if that's not enough?"

*Then we grow. We learn. We adjust. That is what growth is, Mu Chen. Not a straight line from seed to harvest. A process of adaptation. Of response. Of becoming.* The Vine pulsed — warmth, affection, ancient patience. *You have grown more in forty days than most farmers grow in a lifetime. You have grown bonds, and partnerships, and trust. You have grown a family — of plants, of soldiers, of impossible things that refused to die.*

"You call us a family?"

*I would. Yes.* A pause. *The Prima is your sister-plant. The Seed is your... sibling, I suppose. The Gardeners are your ancestors. Qin Shuang is...*

"Don't."

*Qin Shuang is the person you are growing alongside. The one who makes the growth worthwhile.*

I didn't have an answer for that.

---

General Liu came to the farm that afternoon.

She arrived alone — no guards, no delegation, no political entourage. Just her, on horseback, riding through the ash and the recovery like it was any other day.

"Mu Chen," she said, dismounting at the gate.

"General."

"The Wall Guard Council has completed its review of the Tide. Final casualties: two hundred and seventeen killed. Fourteen settlements overrun. Eastern gate structurally compromised." She paused. "And one farm on the outer perimeter that killed two Class 5 entities and held the line when the wall would have fallen."

"I had help."

"You had plants. You grew them. You trusted them. You used them in ways no one had ever imagined." She walked to the eastern boundary and looked at the Vine — thirty meters of impossible growth, now with a partial canopy. "The Council wants to give you a title. A formal designation. Something that reflects what you've done."

"I'm a farmer."

"You're a lot more than that. The Council wants to call you the Wall Guard's first — and only — *Agricultural Commander*. A rank equivalent to a Centurion. Official status, official authority, official recognition."

"Why?"

"Because when the wall needed saving, you grew something no weapon could match. Because you fed your soldiers with plants that killed Behemoths. Because you turned a farm into a fortress and a fortress into a home." She turned to face me. "The Council wants the world to know that farming matters. That growth matters. That the things you plant and tend and care for can be more powerful than any sword."

"I'm a farmer," I repeated. "I just wanted to grow things."

"And look what you grew." She gestured at the farm — the Vine, the Prima, the recovering barriers, the soldiers scattered around the clearing, the sisters sitting together by the medical tent. "You grew a miracle. Maybe more than one."

---

**[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION — EVENING]**

**Official Recognition: Wall Guard Council**

**Designation: Agricultural Commander (provisional)**

**Rank equivalent: Centurion (pending confirmation)**

**Authority: Full command of Primordial Farm operations**

**Status: First Agricultural Commander in Wall Guard history**

**Additional awards:**
- "Wall's Edge" commendation (Corporal Huang, Private Liang, Zhao, Wen — posthumous)
- Strategic Asset Protection Order (Commander Wei, personal)
- Research Collaboration Agreement (Dr. Lin, Agricultural Division)
- Formal apology (Director Han, Commerce League — coerced)

**Mu Chen's official response: "I'm still a farmer."**

**Everyone else's response: "Sure you are."**

---

That evening, I sat with Qin Shuang at the farm's eastern boundary.

The sun — the permanent gray bruise that passed for daylight — was setting, painting the recovering farm in shades of amber and shadow. The Vine loomed above us, its canopy casting dappled shade. The Seed glowed softly in my hands.

"I have to decide," I said. "About the Seed. About the Bridge."

"I know."

"If I plant it — if I try to grow the Second Garden — there's a chance I fail. Like the seventh farmer. Maybe worse."

"And if you don't?"

"Then I wait. For someone else. For the ninth farmer. For whoever comes after me." I looked at the Seed. "But Qin Yue found this for a reason. The Gardeners have been waiting twelve hundred years. The Vine has been waiting three thousand."

"You don't have to do it alone."

"I know. That's what makes it different from the seventh farmer. He tried to do it alone. He commanded instead of partnered." I looked at her — gray eyes, dark hair, the same stubborn jaw as her sister. "I've been partnering with plants since I got here. Why should the Seed be different?"

"Because the Seed is bigger. The Seed is the Bridge. The Seed is..." She paused. Found the word. "The Seed is the future. Everything hinges on it. Everything you've built — the Vine, the Prima, this farm, these people — it all leads to this."

"That's terrifying."

"That's also true."

We sat in silence for a while. The Vine pulsed overhead. The Prima glowed in the northwest corner. The Seed pulsed in my hands like a heartbeat.

"I'm going to plant it," I said finally.

"When?"

"Tomorrow. I need one more night to rest, to prepare, to say goodbye to the idea of a normal life." I smiled. "Then tomorrow, I plant the Seed. And we grow the Bridge."

"And if it fails?"

"If it fails, we try again. That's what farming is. You plant. Things grow. Sometimes they die. You plant again."

She looked at me. And in her gray eyes, I saw something I hadn't expected — not fear, not doubt, but *pride*. Raw, unguarded, overwhelming pride.

"You're impossible," she said.

"Fourth time."

"I'm keeping count."

She put her head on my shoulder. We sat together at the edge of the farm, at the edge of the known world, with a thirty-meter Vine above us and a Seed in my hands and the promise of something impossible waiting to be grown.

Tomorrow, I would plant the Seed.

Tonight, I was just a farmer, sitting with the person he loved, watching the sun set over the only home he'd ever truly had.

It was enough.

For now, it was enough.
